desc:
Turning and bending, the Old East Road leads around a few sudden depressions or
rocky heights, which are covered with boulders. Small bushes have grown so near
to the road that their branches already touch the ancient stones and a few roots
have begun work apart the worn pavement.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Many cracks cover the ancient road. The pavement is broken in many places and
small patches of thin grass have taken root between the stones. The road itself
climbs over a small hill, only to descend quite fast into a slight depression.
Hills dug by moles can be spotted on the northern side of the road.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road runs between two ancient oaken trees. Their huge crowns span high above
and cast a huge shadow on the ground. Countless days of sun have bleached all of
the stones that seldom get touched by shadows. Fallen leaves, mixed with
rain-water, have turned the stones below the trees darker over time, making the
road seem to be made of a different kind of stone in that place.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Here the road runs east-west. You see that wagon wheels have dug deep ruts into
the road that are filled with water from recent heavy rains. There are many
potholes, and the road is bordered by high plains to the south, and a light
forest to the north.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Road to Fornost runs north and south, straight as an arrow, completely
unhindered by the terrain. Rushes grow in parallel ditches alongside the
slightly elevated road, whose time-worn surface is made from carefully arranged
flat stone slabs. Tangled bushes are visible far to the west, beyond a flat
field of tall grasses.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road bends west around a very large boulder, obviously it was easier to just
build a bit of extra road over trying to break it apart. Some farmsteads can be
seen in the distance to the north on both sides of the road. A gap in the road
where one of the slabs that makes up the road's surface have somehow been
removed, has been filled in by packed earth.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The road makes a sharp bend here to avoid an area of dense vegetation and rocky
outcroppings to the north. A small paved track leaves the road heading west
towards a farmstead in the distance. The road itself is well-paved and can
easily handle two wagons going in opposite directions without neither of them
having to skirt the ditches that border the road on either side.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The state of the Road to Fornost is surprisingly good, a testimony to the skill
of those who built the roads of Arnor hundreds of years ago. The road is wide
and paved with flat stones expertly fit together, leaving only tiny earth-filled
gaps between them. The road is bordered on one side by dense bushes and on the
other by more accessible fields.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
A wide, paved road runs from south to north through the surrounding fields. The
road is slightly raised, and slopes down on either side into shallow but wide
ditches that are lush with grasses, suggesting that the soil there stays damp
long after a rain. Between the road and the ditches, the downwards slope pauses
briefly for a narrow footpath that follows the course of the road; it does not
appear to be heavily used, with scrub bushes and tall weed stalks growing up
through its gravel surface. The road itself is mostly level, paved with flat
stone blocks that still have remnants of a type of cement filling gaps between.
name: The Lane

desc:
A wide, raised road runs from south to north here. It is paved with flat stones,
large and naturally-shaped except for their smooth tops, but the gaps between
them create a bumpy surface for carts and carriages. Here and there the remnants
of a type of cement fills in these gaps, but everywhere else it appears to have
long since been worn away by the weather. A narrow dirt farm track joins this
paved lane from both the west and the east, and the stones at the edge of the
lane at this intersection have been worn down, their edges crumbling from
repeated traffic over the bump down to the dirt track beyond.
name: The Lane Meets a Farm Track

desc:
A wide, paved road runs from south to north here, between tall hedgerows. It is
paved with wide, flat blocks that fit together closely enough to leave only
small gaps. Its surface is raised, sloping down on each side to shallow but wide
ditches sunk down below the level of the surrounding fields. The ditches are
grassy but are becoming overgrown, filled with dead weed stalks and scrub bushes
growing densely in its low passage where the soil stays moist for long periods
after a rain. Past the ditches on either side of the road tall hedgerows rise
up, blocking passage to the fields that can be seen beyond.
name: The Lane between Hedges

desc:
A raised road runs through this area, paved with irregularly-shaped stone
blocks. The blocks fit together closely, with small gaps between; some sort of
concrete fills these gaps in some places, but has completely worn away
everywhere else. The road surface is level but no longer smooth, as not all of
the paving-stones are flat on top, and the resulting unevenness makes for a
bumpy ride for the carts and wagons that follow this road. The edges of the road
are gravel, wide enough to have once been used as walking paths, but now these
gravel pathways are blocked by scrub bushes and young whips of trees, and the
easiest passage is along the road itself. Hedges rise up past the edges of the
road, blocking access to the overgrown farm fields that can be seen beyond.
name: The Lane between Hedges

desc:
This raised road is paved with large, flat stone blocks. Here and there the
stone blocks are starting to crumble in place, leading to low spots where the
top layers of stone have disappeared. Some of the stone blocks look as if they
have been replaced entirely, the new blocks fitted inexpertly in the gaps left
behind. A pile of old, broken paving-stones is lying in one of the ditches that
borders this road, the detritus left from these road repairs. Past the ditches
tall, tangled hedges rise up, blocking movement far off the road. These hedges
look like they were once uniform, but now have tall weed stalks and young trees
poking through them.
name: The Lane between Hedges

desc:
A raised, paved road turns from west to north here. It is paved with flat,
somewhat rounded stones that fit together as closely as their shapes allow; the
gaps are filled with rock dust and fine grit, giving no space for weeds to grow.
The road surface is mostly level, but some of the paving-stones have sunken over
time, creating bumps that can jolt a cart or wagon as it passes. The edges of
the road slope down to wide, shallow ditches that are overgrown with tall
grasses and scrub bushes. This brush becomes more dense to the south and east,
where taller bushes rise up in great thickets that block movement in those
directions.
name: The Lane

desc:
A wide, paved road turns from south to east here. The road is raised slightly
above the surrounding land, and it slopes down to shallow ditches on either
side. It is paved with large, uniform stones that are relatively flat on top,
creating a smooth surface for the passage of carts and carriages. There appear
to have once been gravel paths on either side of the road, above the ditches;
over time, however, these gravel surfaces have eroded into the ditches, leaving
only a bump as the sides of the road slope downwards. To the north and west,
beyond the ditches alongside the road, tall shrubs rise up, tangled and thorny
enough to prevent passage in those directions.
name: The Lane

desc:
A road runs from south to north here, its wide roadbed raised above the fields
that border it. The road is paved with wide stones, naturally shaped but flat
enough on top to create a smooth and uniform surface. Here and there corners of
these paving stones have crumbled or broken, leaving small gaps that risk a
turned ankle for those walking along the road, but are not large enough to
create a bumpy journey for carriages or carts that follow its passage. Off each
side of the road, a short slope leads down to a wide ditch that is hard to see
due to the brush and grasses that grow tall enough to appear level with the
grasses in the fields beyond. To the east and west, beyond the ditches and
fields, tall lines of tangled brush rise up, blocking movement.
name: The Lane

desc:
A wide road runs through the fields here. The road bed is raised, paved with
flat, square stones that fit closely together. Here and there patches of some
sort of cement fill the gaps between the paving stones, securing them in place
and smoothing the road surface. The edges of the road slope down to a gravel
walking path that runs along each side before continuing down into shallow
ditches. The gravel paths look unused: tall herbaceous weeds poke through, and
the gravel surface is eroding into the ditches. To the east and south the fields
next to the road quickly give way to obstructive lines of brush, tangled and
dense enough to block movement.
name: The Lane

desc:
A raised road, paved with large, flat stones runs from west to east here. The
paving stones fit together closely, although in places there are wider gaps left
from damage to the stones or a shifting of the stones over time. In these gaps
the surface beneath can be glimpsed: a packed mix of fine gravel and rock dust,
forming a stable base that allows for drainage. The edges of the road slope down
to wide ditches before rising again into the fields on either side of the road.
In these ditches grow different grasses than in the fields beyond, with larger,
taller stems and a spreading rather than clump-forming habit.
name: The Lane

desc:
The north curb of the lane stretches out into a disused dirt track, while the
main road continues east and west. In the cracks of the lane, wild oats grow
stubbornly among the horse dung and gravel. On the either side of the road, a
shallow ditch grows thick with rustling bulrush and reeds. To the south, rolling
fields of barley grow well-tended before some rather rugged hills. To the north,
fields of oats thrive in neat, flat parcels.
name: The Lane

desc:
A hilly arm of Chetwood presses into the path of the road from the southeast,
forcing the road to press along a decrepit stone wall to the north and west. The
gravel shoulder of the road completely disappears, a problem little aided by the
derelict conditions of the main paving stones as it rounds the bend. Over the
wall, fields of alfalfa grow green and hale, stretching to the vista.
name: The Lane

desc:
The western shoulder of the lane has been choked off by loose dirt and rocks
that have tumbled down from a sloping field. A great view of the land stretches
up, bunched with juniper scrub, gnarly blackthorn, and sparse grasses. A hilly
arm of Chetwood jabs its way into the road from the east, forcing it to narrow
as it bends around to the northeast.
name: The Lane

desc:
A road of cracked paving stones is bordered by loose gravel on either side. In
places, the cracks in the pavement are so severe that it is easier to pull a
cart along the gravel than it is to traverse the ridges and fissures created by
the cracks. Hills rise to either side: the Bree-land forest looming in the east,
and a slick ascent to a barren ridge in the west.
name: The Lane

desc:
The raised lane is lined on each side by long, narrow ditches and leads exactly
to the north and south. The road is paved with smooth, polished stones and is
still in great condition. However, huge blocks of stone are beginning to move,
most likely the passed ages finally start to take their tribute.
name: The Lane

desc:
The raised road runs in a straight line to the north and south and is in good
condition. Huge trees with big trunks and hard bark line the road beside long,
narrow ditches. In between the trees lie many large, white boulders. Smooth
paving stones in the road are beginning to shift from the heavy daily traffic.
name: The Lane

desc:
Small grasses grow all over the nearly flat meadow. Spots of different coloured
grasses are the only change in colour as far as the eye can see. To the north
the grass gets lower and slightly darker. A well-paved and well-travelled road
lines the eastern horizon.
name: Open Field

desc:
The raised road runs straight to the north and to the south, lying in a constant
shade of ancient trees. The trees have bordered this road for countless years
and their crowns rise high above, forming archways big enough even for giants.
Long, narrow ditches are dug into the ground between the road and the trees.
name: The Lane

desc:
The bark of the ancient trees lining the road is smooth and very hard. The road
itself seems to be in very good condition. It is raised slightly above the
surrounding landscape, and paved with smooth stones. Long, narrow ditches are
dug into the ground, running parallel to the road.
name: The Lane

desc:
Running to the north and to the south and being bordered by ancient, huge trees
which form a giant archway over the road, the lane looks more like an avenue for
kings from old and forgotten days; not as the path of travellers, adventurers,
and merchants that it is today. The road is slightly raised above the
surrounding land, and paved with smooth stones.
name: The Lane

desc:
A large paved road is here, worn down from many years of use. Countless
travellers and wheels have left their marks on the surface of the road. Dust
hovers over the ground from constant use, and its firm surface allows for easy
travel.
name: Large Road

desc:
Paw and hoof prints from the occasional wolf or deer run along the loamy,
northern edges of the Old East Road, sometimes crossing the wide throughway.
Nearby and to the south, a large hedge separates this road from a smaller,
fainter trail. Beyond the trail, gently rolling hillsides mark the horizon.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road continues in its east-west orientation. A short path branches off the
road and leads to what appears to be an inn to the south, situated between a
thick thicket on the east and a large thicket on the west. The dense forest to
the north makes travel impossible.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road divides the Chetwood forest into northern and southern halves, as a
viridescent cloak girdled by a tan leather belt. A tangle of brushwood, unkempt
hedge, and low buckthorn lie immediately to the south, while a curious rift in
the greenery to the north beckons passage into the depths of the wood.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Some of the trees covering the hillsides in this area are not found in other
parts of the forest. There are still many pines, but here and there the
occasional alder can be seen as well. In the denser portions of the wood, the
ground is covered by a thick undergrowth of ferns.
name: Lightly Forested Hillsides

desc:
The road travels east and west, connecting the village of Bree with the hidden
valley of Rivendell, far beyond the eastern horizon. This section of the road
has been well worn by the many travellers from distant lands who traverse it. To
the north, a coppice scars the otherwise lush and verdant Chetwood. To the
south, a faint trail leads into the wood.
name: Old East Road

desc:
At the edges of this narrow path, the trees are quite large and grow in a moist,
rich soil. The path leads north to the Old East Road, branches off into a
smaller trail to the east, and opens up southwards to some grassy downs before
curving towards the west.
name: Small Path

desc:
The road is worn away from vigorous use. Small bushes line the edges of the road
with the Chetwood forest rising behind them. A small path leads toward a
clearing in the distance to the north. To the south, some rolling downs are
barely visible through the scattered trees.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Old East Road is wide and well-worn here. Frequent foot, hoof, and cart
traffic has made the earth hard and smooth. A dense spinney to the north
obscures any sightline into Chetwood forest. To the south, a broad break in the
hedge leads into the southern part of the wood.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road runs east and west, separating the Chetwood forest to the north and
south. The woods get increasingly dense to the south, limiting any access to the
forest. Far to the west, the village of Bree, particularly its high hill, can be
seen.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road continues its way east and west, dividing the Chetwood forest to the
north and south as it goes. The forest has been thinned out to the north and
small piles of brushwood border the road. To the south the trees rise up fairly
high and a dense hedgerow blocks travel in that direction.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Made of packed soil and dirt, the road is both wide and frequently travelled by
wagons, horses and feet. Over twelve feet wide, the road travels straight both
east and west, bordered by grass and small bushes. To the west the road
intersects with the Greenway and towards the east a large hedge spreads afar
bordering Bree city.
name: Old East Road

desc:
This is the crossing of the ancient Old East Road and the Greenway, the equally
old road that runs north and south. A few trees and thick bushes stand next to
the crossing. In the northeast corner of the crossing, an ancient, massive
boulder can be seen. The Greenway runs in a straight line southward, and cuts
the grass-covered lands to its sides in two. The Old East Road bends in the west
and quickly vanishes from sight.
name: Old East Road Meets Greenway

desc:
The narrow dirt track underfoot is rough and stony as the ground begins to climb
toward a tall bush-crowned hill far to the north. To the east, between
boulder-encrusted hillsides is the entrance to lush, grass-carpeted valley.
Grassy meadows broken by clusters of bushes and trees decorate the soft, rolling
terrain in the south and west.
name: Narrow Track up a Hill

desc:
The field is brindled: lumpy streaks of charcoal spread across the brittle,
flax-coloured grasses. In addition to the charred landscape, the ashy aroma
lingering in the air is a clear sign that this field was recently burned. A mill
looms in the eastern distance, standing in relief against an otherwise flat
horizon.
name: Burned Field behind a Mill

desc:
A seemingly disorganized mesh of white forms the home of spider here. White,
fuzzy balls, the wrapped and preserved food for the arachnid, are woven into the
fabric of the web. Newly-caught insects struggle against the sticky constraints,
their future doom unknown to them.
name: Sticky Cave

desc:
Dry, yellow grasses stretch toward the east before rising into a dense, tall
tangle of prairie. The relatively flat ground rises quickly to the west. The
western hillside is rock-strewn, but in between the boulders and bushes are wide
swaths of grassland producing pathways more navigable than the unsteady ground
farther south.
name: Grassland below Some Rocky Hills

desc:
The meadow is thick here, and the flowers and grasses stretch toward the sky
reaching surprising heights. In some places, the goldenrods and switchgrasses
stand taller than the farmers and occasional traveller who move through these
parts. The dense foliage keeps the soil cool and damp, and the smell of wet
earth lingers in the air.
name: Tall Meadow

desc:
South of the trail, the grasses rise quickly. Bright green-yellow clumps of low
grass give way to taller, lacy brown stalks and thorny thistles. Black and red
burnets and fat, fuzzy bumblebees dart among the flowers. Farther south and
west, the meadow thickens and grows even higher.
name: Meadow by a Trail

desc:
The rocky path extends east and west, dotted with clusters of blue-green grass
and rough stones, some large enough to trip an unobservant traveller. Near the
northern edge of the trail, a rough-edged gully gashes the landscape, and the
ground abruptly sinks more than a few feet below the level of the path. The
depression continues many paces to the north before rising sharply again. On the
opposite side, a vivid green pasture stretches toward the horizon.
name: Rough Path beside a Gully

desc:
A tall mill looms nearby and beyond, farther to the west, the fields begin to
rise and become low, lightly-forested hillsides. An overgrown trail stretches
toward the east, paralleled by a gully on the north and a thick, lumpish hedge
to the south. The mill is in a near-dilapidated state: dry rot has begun to eat
into the lattice frame of the sails, a few dozen shingles are missing from the
roof, and ivy smothers the upper windows.
name: Before a Large Mill

desc:
The trail takes a wide bend to the west, crossing in front of the tree line and
bordered on the north by an increasingly shallow gully. The low grasses that
line the southern edge of the trail gradually rise into a tall meadow to the
southwest, and low hills roll along the otherwise smooth, western horizon.
name: Bend in an Overgrown Trail

desc:
The trail branches off to the east, running behind a small house before merging
into the well-worn Fornost road. A few oak trees bordering the western edge of
the road extend their branches overhead, but taper off farther north. Puffs of
sheep fescue freckle the trail ahead, and the tallest blades sway softly in the
breeze.
name: Intersection on a Rugged Trail

desc:
Someone, most likely a nearby farmer, while having once completed the strenuous
job of laying a path with uncountable stone slabs, has now let weeds grow
between them and some slabs are barely visible beneath the dirt. The path trails
closely to the fence on the northern side, while splitting into a fork further
to the west. Far to the east, the path leads joins the Road to Fornost.
name: Path of Tiny Stone Slabs

desc:
A path runs through a wide open field of swaying grass. The path seems to see
regular use, as it's mostly free of rocks and weeds. It is laid with many small
slabs of stone, which no doubt help with keeping it in good repair, and keeps
weeds at bay. The path leads to a field of brush to the north, and to a larger
road to the east. It also continues into the distance to the west.
name: Path of Tiny Stone Slabs

desc:
Having been allowed to grow wild and unchecked, the tangled bushes on these
fields grow right up to a wooden fence to the west. Where the bushes don't
dominate, knee-high grass flourishes, and not a path or trail crosses these wide
fields.
name: Brushy Corner

desc:
Tall, wooden fence posts frame this brush to the north, and south, and such is
the suffocating nature of the vines, that no tall plants rise above the
withering mass. No trail can be discerned under the black thorns below, and the
vines wriggle and snap as if searching for victims.
name: Blackened Brush

desc:
The thorny brush is quite light here, with ample sunlight reaching the ground
during most of the year. Several tall trees have grown through the low canopy,
and their branches provide a resting spot for migratory birds, squirrels, and
snakes. Far to the north, a wooden fence pokes its beams above the brush.
name: Thorny Brush

desc:
The ground is rough and uneven. In many places, it is difficult to find secure
footing and each step sends smaller stones rolling down the hillside. Despite
the rugged landscape, stunning wildflowers bloom between the jagged rocks,
freckling the otherwise brown and grey landscape with a palette of yellow,
orange, and purple colour.
name: Rock-strewn Hill

desc:
Despite their unremarkable height, these hills are harsh. Abrupt and
surprisingly steep scarps are carved into their sides, and the rocky ground
makes slipping an almost inevitable event. The air is dry and dusty; not much
grows here except the occasional large sagebrush, adding a welcome seagreen tint
to the mostly chalky surroundings.
name: Rugged Hills

desc:
Ascending this hill takes a lot of effort as it slopes steeply and continuously
from its base to its summit. From the peak, a big mill can be seen clearly in
the far eastern distance, and it is possible to watch the sails spin in response
to the breath of the wind.
name: Sharply-rising Slope

desc:
Standing atop this tall hill offers an unobstructed panorama of the surrounding
agricultural lands. Southeast of here lies an orchard and a small farm and
beyond that, an old dirt road. To the northeast, the tall sails of a mill can
barely be distinguished from the lush pasture stretching behind it. Directly
north, the hills dip lower and become more rugged, specked with large rocks and
thick, thorny bushes.
name: Tall Hill

desc:
Standing atop this low hill, it is possible to see the tree line along the road
far to the east. The hill dips gradually to the west and another, taller hill
rises beyond the hollow. To the north and east is a perilous slope, pocked with
large boulders and the serrated branches of low, thorny shrubs.
name: Steep Slope

desc:
Rough and clawing greenery gives way to jutting bedrock as these hills rise to
rocky cliffs. Only a few gnarly weeds manage to thrive between the cracks in the
rocks on a ridge that drops sharp and rocky. The ground at the bottom of the
cliffs is full of yellow rocks strewn between shrubs. As the land extends, it
becomes lush and green.
name: Rocky Precipice

desc:
These hills are green mounds of turf, poked through with large boulders. Under
many crevices grow crooked juniper trees, clawing at anything that comes near.
The ground is firm, and the thick grass grows well. The vantage from this high
land gives an outstanding view of the verdant fields south and west.
name: Green Mounds

desc:
The field slopes gently upward to the east, cresting into a tall hill in the far
distance. Elsewhere, the landscape is grassy, still, and flat. Here and there, a
bush or a boulder appears as a dark lump on the otherwise featureless
countryside.
name: Gently-rising Field

desc:
This broad grassland sinks down, low and almost boggy. Stalwart bulrushes and
yellow-flowered brooklime abound among the lush figwort clumps, giving the
ground a thick green covering. The air is thick with moisture, and swarms of
small gnats fly about as the plants are disturbed. The land is so low that on
the east and south sides, small dirt cliffs rise to the surrounding land, brown
and crumbling. Tiny puddles are formed in the dirt, providing a drinking source
for insects and small animals.
name: Wet Grassland

desc:
Thick grass is divided by a series of lines of corn cockle on this rolling
plain. Curving from east to south, the loose, long, plants billow their purple
flowers from ditches in a lazy wave. The grass is kept mostly low, but some
patches have managed to evade cutting, growing tall and thick.
name: Thick Grass

desc:
This field lay broken as if hammered from above by a giant. The ground is
carpeted with sedge and sweet briar on the angles, firm but crooked, giving a
tilt to all travel. Some large rocks begin to appear on the southern edge, and
to the east the land becomes soggy. To the west the land rises slightly,
blocking the view of the top.
name: Broken Field

desc:
On this vast grassy field is a circle of majestic trees fencing in a round
plateau. The ground is flourishing with bunches of sedge, and as it reaches a
gentle drop off to the east and south, it becomes thin and rocky allowing a
grand view of wild lands, rocky and bushy.
name: Vast Field

desc:
A shelf of hard soil and loose shrubbery juts out from a prominent plateau to
the north. The land angles in a grand vista, giving an easy eyeshot east and
south where the earth descends to greener fields. The air is filled with an
earthy smell as the wind blows the soil down a low rise to the north.
name: Grand Vista

desc:
This field sinks deep below the level of the surrounding fields, giving it
little sunlight throughout the day. The soil is hard, and exposes the underlying
bedrock in many places. Some downy birch trees grow across the land, gnarly and
drooping, sharing the land with spindly cock's foot grass.
name: Hollow Field

desc:
This land appears to have been recently cleared and half ploughed, although no
crop has been planted. The resulting fescue grows in straight rows, nature's
quick reclamation of the land, as obvious as the creeping greenery that is thick
and wild south and west.
name: Recently Cleared Field

desc:
A great field of barley is abandoned, it growing savagely against long and lanky
corn cockle that attempts to choke it out. Crookedly splitting the field is a
dried creek bed, its banks overhung with buckthorn, the shiny, black branches
dropping their small, shrivelled berries at will.
name: Choking Corn Cockle

desc:
Gravel fills a depression in this rocky expanse. Too steep to climb out to the
north or west, the ground is full of grey and black sharp stones. Cockle and
oxeye grow sparingly among the rocks, giving way to true grasses on the lower
points of this field as the ground becomes loamy southeast.
name: Depression of Rocks

desc:
A light breeze wafts through this verdant meadow, blowing the short grasses with
a gentle touch. A large moss covered boulder sits at a low spot and provides
protection for a short leaved butcher's broom, stiff and fresh. The rest of the
land pitches slowly to the boulder, making it the focal point. Elsewhere the
meadow is well grazed, the grasses being short and trim.
name: Verdant Meadow

desc:
Long, wavy strands of fescue flutter about this vigorous field. Grasses and
bushes strangle each other for sunlight, and the mushy gault sucks the plants
down. A sparse set of tall oak trees grows thick here, providing a resting spot
for small animals.
name: Vigorous Field

desc:
Strips of straggly, flat grass weave and wind their way through scattered rocks
and large, overgrown hunks of earth. The southern part of this field is so
filled with the haphazard rocks that there is no clear path through them. The
smell of earth is abundant, and the cold stones dampen any sound.
name: Straggly Grass

desc:
Fog grass and sedge billow at any slight wind on this flourishing upland. The
vista gives a grand view of the fields west and south. To the north the land
drops off sharply, exposing a sunken field. East, green bushes grow wild and
large, hindering any but the smallest of animals from travelling there.
name: Flourishing Upland

desc:
Cocksfoot and foxtail grasses stretch over this shabby field, waving slowly in
the breeze. Signs of grazing are evident from the bent and eaten herbage, tufts
of fleece, and ordure. To the north, the land rises into an upland. To the east,
large, wild thickets impede travel.
name: Shabby Field

desc:
Green and silver grass bends to the wind on this vast, flat plain. The earth is
rich with the smell of life, verdant, and green, and springs up underfoot. The
grass itself is wide leaved, and furry, and the heads of spikey flowers droop
under their own weight.
name: Flat Plain

desc:
The land here has been forgotten for a very long time, and wild flora has taken
over every square inch. Most of the plants are brambles, sticking small thorns
in every direction; so thick that the only way to exit from this vexing land is
to the east.
name: Field Enclosed by Brambles

desc:
Scattered wild privet grows thick on the eastern and southern edges of this wild
field. The dull green, lance-shaped leaves and small, white, cloyingly sweet
flowers contrast with the clover and thistle that spread through the rest of
this land.
name: Wild Privet

desc:
This rough land is thick with prickly thistle and dandelion, grabbing on to any
passing thing. To the west and east, dark green bushes grow shiny and tall,
hemming the land in on both sides. To the southeast, large bushes fight with
shorter, dull green privet for command of the domain. A sharp, sweet smell
riddles the air, luring lazy honeybees to purple and yellow flowers.
name: Rough Land

desc:
Below an imposing escarpment, a pitched morass of rocks and shrubbery bound
southward, transitioning to lush grasses. The escarpment is dark yellow and
stained with watery, rust-coloured rivulets. To the west, the land becomes thick
with tall, dark green, shiny bushes, and is impassable.
name: Below an Imposing Escarpment

desc:
North, an overhanging ridge stands dramatically over this rocky land, dark
yellow and wrinkled. The base is thick with fallen rock, gradually spreading out
and intermingling with small scrub pines and shiny, dark green bushes. Further
out, the land becomes flat and is overtaken by hardy grasses.
name: Overhanging Ridge

desc:
Dense swards of fine-leaved grasses fight with erect, yellow-flowered ragwort on
this field that starts on rocky slopes in the north. Continuing south, the land
becomes rich and butcher's broom becomes prevalent: the stringent, leafy bushes
reaching waist-high.
name: Among Some Ragwort

desc:
This field pitches down east from a rocky hill. The land is covered by short
turf on the west, and gradually becomes more lush, growing thick grass, large
shrubs and scrub trees. The finality of the plant life crawls up to a stout
fence, enveloping the white boards in dark green.
name: Pitching Field

desc:
The surrounding field consists of low hills that level off in the east and rise
sharply toward the west. The western slope is uninviting. Giant, jagged boulders
and thorny bushes cover the hillside, and the ground is littered with smaller,
loose rocks that call into doubt the possibility for stable footing.
name: Steeply-sloping Field

desc:
The grasses are lower here, and wildflowers large and small grow in abundance.
Bright corncockle, pastel cornflower and cranesbill, and sunny daffodils paint
the landscape in vibrant colour and contrast pleasingly with the green and umber
grasses.
name: Sprawling Meadow

desc:
Here and there a lone oak rises from the tall grasses, and playful meadowlarks
chirp as they glide between the branches. On especially sunny days, a subtle
smell of mint and hay lingers on the breeze among the sun-warmed stalks. The
meadow stretches farther west and to the south, a dense cluster of trees mark
the edge of an orchard.
name: Sweet-smelling Meadow

desc:
Wide, shallow depressions are carved into the dirt floor of this small cave,
their paths meandering from the opening into the dark recesses of the interior.
Tree roots form living stalagtites, their tendrils grasping for dirt in the open
air of the cave.
name: Slender Cave

desc:
The southern dirt road narrows here into a faint and rocky trail and continues
north. A sparse tree line borders the western edge of the trail, beyond which a
tall meadow stretches into the hilly distance. Unkempt, low bushes encroach from
either side of this fading footpath.
name: Overgrown Trail

desc:
Sweet vernal-grass cover this honeyed land. A branch of the old farm track runs
north and south through, untended and overgrown. To the east thickets of elder
fight with strangling clematis. To the west, a sturdy fence holds back the wild
from a well tended field.
name: Old Farm Track

desc:
Two dirt tracks cross here. Well worn ruts and short, tough grass give evidence
of the constant usage they see. The land around is planted with small fields of
onions, garlic, carrots, and beets, all hemmed with short, thorny lattice. To
the west, a large farm house stands, steadily guarding the land.
name: Crossing of the Farm Tracks

desc:
In this sparse, rocky field brome and melic grow shaggily, giving a good glimpse
of the soil. A dirt track runs through the land, north and south, the cart ruts
shallow on small rocks and gravel. To the east, dark elder thickets grow with
abandon, eventually becoming impassable. In the west, a white-painted fence
holds back the brush, guarding rows of tended oats.
name: Sparse Rocky Field

desc:
Cart tracks wind their way through this great meadow of fescue, hard thicket,
pimpernel, and honeysuckle. The flowers give the land a deep orange over top of
the green of the sedge. On the track, hardy grasses grow short and tough, not
letting the wagon wheels crush their life. In the west, a white-painted fence
guards rows of tended crops. The track continues north and south.
name: Old Dirt Track

desc:
A wide, grassy field grows thick, thorny creeping bent and soft sweetbriar.
Smooth, grey barked whitebeam trees dot the eastern side of the field, which is
split in two by an old dirt track. The track's deep ruts left from heavy wagon
wheels have crushed the cobble stones that once paved this track into rocks and
gravel.
name: Old Dirt Track

desc:
Elder, sweetbriar, and harebell form a rough, blue-black sedge in this field.
The land is forked by two tracks of heavy wagon wheels carving through the
topsoil, exposing chunks of flattened stones beneath. To the east, the land is
home to patches of small whitebeam trees, compact and domed.
name: Fork in an Old Dirt Track

desc:
Cart tracks wind their way through this dirt track as it passes by the ruin of a
stately stone farmhouse that is nearly engulfed in choking thornbushes. An iron
structure stands at what might have once been the gate, but has now crumbled
into rust. Out from the ruins and the track, the land turns from thick brambles
to a large field full of long-stemmed cow parsley and bunched ribwort.
name: Old Dirt Track near a House

desc:
An old dirt track road runs from west to east here. The track surface is uneven
in places, with humps and shallow depressions breaking up the otherwise flat
passage. Some of the depressions are marred by groups of narrow channels cutting
across the road, suggesting that water draining along an almost imperceptible
slope has resulted in erosion. Here and there short clumps of grasses have begun
to grow in the road itself, eking out a tenuous existence in the hard packed
dirt. At the edges of the road similarly short grass quickly gives way to taller
blades and clumps as the soil becomes looser in the surrounding fields, which
continue to the north and south.
name: Farm Track

desc:
This dirt track is not entirely flat: some areas are slightly raised, forming
bumps and low mounds, and other spots are low, holding water when it rains. Here
and there rocks stick up from the track, large smooth stones that are too large
to easily dig out. Along the edges of the track are piles of rocks that were
smaller and easier to remove, dug and tossed aside to create a more uniform
surface upon which to travel. Some low-growing weeds have managed to establish
in the hard packed dirt in the centre and edges of the track, growing slowly
thanks to the poorly-aerated soil. Tall grasses quickly emerge off the track to
the north and south, and continue as far as the eye can see, while to the east
the dirt track joins a larger road paved with flat stones.
name: Farm Track

desc:
This area lacks mature trees or even tree stumps, suggesting that it was cleared
at some point in the past. Tall grasses and very low heath and bramble bushes
grow here instead, struggling to rise above shade cast by the surrounding
grasses. Mixed in with the grasses are tall, dead stems from a previous year's
annual weeds, some with seed pods still attached to the terminal ends. To the
east a long, straight line of bushes rises above the surrounding fields. The
bushes grow closely together, forming a dense hedgerow, but do not appear to
have been cared for in some time: vines twine through them, shading and thinning
out parts of the hedgerow, and young birch trees have begun to poke up through
the top of the hedge, reaching for the sky.
name: Overgrown Hedge in the Fields

desc:
This area looks like it was cleared at some point in the past, as it lacks any
mature trees or even large stumps. Instead it is covered with thick, messy
clumps of grasses, the green blades from this year's growth intermingled with
drooping brown blades and upright brown flower heads from the growth of previous
years. Within the grasses, hard to see but easy to feel, are many young trees,
unbranched whips with only a year's growth or less. They stick up no higher than
the surrounding grasses, rigid compared to their pliable neighbours, and in some
places are numerous enough to form dense stands. To the east a long, straight
row of bushes rises above the grassy fields, an overgrown hedgerow that suggests
this area was once farmland with defined borders. Beyond the hedge to the east,
a road can be made out in the distance.
name: Overgrown Hedge in the Fields

desc:
Tall, spreading grasses have taken over what appear to be old farm fields in
this area. There are no trees or stumps visible in these fields, although at
irregularly-spaced intervals tall messy shrubs rise from the grasses, forming
dense clumps that are hard to move through but easy to move around. The ruins of
a farmhouse lie in a clear patch, without the walls or roof left intact. It is
little more than a foundation covered with the detritus left from its collapsed
frame: rotted boards and stacked stones mixed with some unidentifiable metal
debris. The landscape is already starting to reclaim the structure, which is
slowly being covered by a sprawling, twining vine. To the west an unbroken line
of tall shrubs grows densely enough to block travel.
name: Ruined House in the Fields

desc:
This area appears to have been cleared of trees at some point in the past,
leaving no trace except the rare stump that has rotted into a shallow depression
with a few forlorn pieces of wood sticking up on the periphery. A multitude of
grass clumps have reclaimed the area, and short scrubby bushes have begun to
appear in groups, forming an irregular mosaic across the fields. Some of the
bushes are taller than others, and to the west a long line of the tallest form a
tangled barrier that prevents movement. To the north a small dirt road can be
seen.
name: Overgrown Farm Fields

desc:
This area appears to have been cleared of trees at some point in the past, as
there are no mature trees or stumps remaining. Tall grasses dominate this area,
broken up by scrub bushes and spindly whips of young trees just starting to
grow. In one area a broad, low depression in the field is visible. This sunken
area has shorter, greener grasses than its surroundings, suggesting that water
collects there when it rains and stays close under the surface for long periods
after, waterlogging the soil and preventing more aggressive plant growth. To the
west the scrub bushes grow taller and more dense, with vines climbing between
and through them to weave an impassable barrier.
name: Overgrown Farm Fields

desc:
Dense stands of gorse bushes and brambles cover the ground here, creating an
obstructive barrier to travel. The brambles are low growing, but thread through
the bases of the taller gorse bushes to produce tall stands of brush that are
uniformly dense from the ground up. Some clumps of grasses and tall weed stalks
poke up through the bushes, both live stems and brown, hollow stems from past
years' growth. An occasional small birch tree has managed to push up through
some of these grassy, brushy patches, reaching just high enough to no longer be
shaded by the plants around it. To the east the brush merges into taller shrubs
that appear to be growing closely together in a straight line, suggesting that
this area may once have been farmland fenced in by a natural hedgerow.
name: Overgrown Hedge in the Brush

desc:
This area appears to have been cleared in the past, as there are few trees here
and none that are mature. Tall, clump-forming grasses are interspersed here with
an almost continuous brushcover, large and small shrubs of various types and
heights. These shrubs grow closely together, their branches intertwining and
forming dense tangles that are difficult to move through. Here and there a
narrow deer trail has been worn through the brush, making travel easier,
although thorny, whip-like branches reach over the trails at regular intervals,
clawing at anything moving past. To the east a road can be made out through the
brush, although the tangled mess between here and there prevents passage in that
direction.
name: Brushy Fields

desc:
Dense brush and the occasional young tree dominate this area. Tall gorse bushes
are mixed with brambles and lower growing heath, creating a tangled mix of
varying heights. Multiple deer trails wind through the brush, the narrow paths
marked by trampled weed stalks and the occasional clump of deer droppings. These
trails criss-cross as they pass each other, avoiding the young trees and the
densest clumps of bushes. To the east and west the brush becomes too tall and
tangled to move through, and to the south a wide, paved road can be seen.
name: Brushy Field

desc:
Tall, thick grass blankets this area, forming a nearly uniform carpet with
clumps of a coarser variety sticking up here and there. Closer to the ground,
everything turns into a messy tangle of new blades mixed with old, dead stems
and flowerheads. Wide stumps from mature trees dot this landscape; some are cut
close to the ground, hidden by the grasses until stumbled upon, while others
stick up just enough that the surrounding grasses fail to reach high enough to
hide them from view. A narrow deer trail threads its way between these stumps, a
ribbon of bare dirt that winds its way through the grassland until it is lost
from view. To the south a narrow dirt road can be seen, and to the north and
west the remnants of what appears to have been a hedgerow block travel.
name: Old Stumps in a Field

desc:
This field is covered with sparse grasses, growing mostly in clumps. Between the
clump-forming grasses a thinner, lower-growing layer of spreading grass mixes
with short woody shrubs, creating a mosaic of varying heights, colours, and
textures. In clear spots there are old stumps left over from the woodlands that
must have been cleared at some point in the past; these stumps are in varying
stages of decay, some with only a hole rotted straight down through the top of
the stump, and others that have little left besides a ring of crumbling wood
rising from the edges of a small depression. To the east a large road can be
seen in the distance, raised and paved with stones, while to the south a small
dirt road cuts through the surrounding fields.
name: Old Stumps in a Field

desc:
The forest was partially cleared here sometime in the distant past. Old, mouldy
stumps still dot the field, and it does not seem like it was used for farming.
Tall trees grow amongst the stumps, and nature has started to reclaim its hold
on the land here. A road runs parallel to the fields to the east, leading both
north and south.
name: Partially Cleared Land

desc:
These fields look like they were once used for farming. The remnants of shallow
ditches can be seen crossing the fields in straight lines, and except for the
grass growing tall and unchecked, the fields have not been reclaimed yet by
trees or larger bushes. A path runs parallel to the field far to the north.
name: Overgrown Field

desc:
A simple dirt farm track extends from the paved lane to the west towards a small
farm visible in the distance to the east. Two ruts have been worn down the
track, attesting to frequent passage of carts or wagons, but otherwise the track
is level and smooth. Short clumps of grasses grow in the hard-packed median
between the cart ruts, struggling in the poorly-aerated soil. An occasional
half-buried stone also sticks up from the median, flat enough to have been left
in place despite the traffic this road must see. At the edges of the road short
grasses give way to taller clumps as the soil becomes looser.
name: Farm Track

desc:
This area is lightly wooded, with many mature trees that are spaced far enough
apart to allow plenty of light to filter through to the ground. This light has
encouraged aggressive growth of the understory: clumps of grasses, wide-leafed
plants on tall stems, and scraggly gorse bushes grow here in abundance. A large
area of this woodland appears to be in the process of being cut down, with
recent-looking stumps and felled tree trunks that have already been stripped of
a majority of their branches. The brush in this area has also been cut down and
left piled by some of the tree trunks, leaving a trampled field with bare dirt
showing. To the south a dirt road can be seen in the distance.
name: Partially-cleared Woods

desc:
This forest was partially cleared sometime in the past. Mixed in with ancient,
looming trees are young ones, and here and there old, rotten stumps form a
feeding ground for small insects and fungus. The forest floor has plenty of
undergrowth, as the canopy is not very dense.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
A mix of birch, pine and rowan trees grow here, spaced far enough apart that
light is able to make it to the ground below. More trees, much younger and
unable to provide any usable shade, grow in the clear spaces between their
mature brethren and alongside a brushy understory. Tall, spreading grasses and
herbaceous plants grow along with scraggly shrubs that are open and spreading in
the intermittent shade cast by the trees above. Parts of these woods have begun
to be cleared: stumps dot this area, and next to them some the felled trunks
still lie, already stripped of their branches. Some of the larger scrub bushes
here have also been cut down, and left in piles next to the stumps.
name: Partially-cleared Woods

desc:
While surrounded by forest to the west and south, this open field offers a clear
view of the sky with no canopy in the way. The forest surrounding this land is
sparse enough that a traveller would have no trouble finding a suitable route
through it.
name: Sheltered Clearing

desc:
This land was ploughed and left to lie fallow, the furrows still visible
underneath the low growth that has sprung up since. Low-growing grasses, sparse
enough in some areas that bare dirt is still visible, mix with the occasional
heath and weedy plants of varying heights. The ground underfoot is still loose
from the plough, not yet packed down by repeated rains, and old weed stalks
stick up from the friable soil where they have been cut under. To the west a
small farmhouse stands, surrounded by a fence and what appear at this distance
to be more fields.
name: Fallow Fields

desc:
These plains are covered with low-growing grasses, mixed with taller clumps of
heath and gorse bushes at irregular intervals. The gorse bushes are still small,
rising barely above the heath, suggesting that they have not been growing here
for long. Some small tree seedlings rise up barely taller than the surrounding
grass, puffs of young pine trees and thin birch whips. No mature trees grow
here, and no stumps are visible, suggesting that this area was cleared at some
point in the past, and maintained as farm fields until recently. The ground
underfoot is relatively flat, but to the east it starts to become more hilly.
name: Low, Grassy Field

desc:
This grassland is dominated by low growing bentgrass, interspersed with taller
clump-forming grasses and short shrubs. Most of the shrubs are heath, but here
and there stands of young gorse bushes are beginning to grow, already shading
out the grasses around them. No mature trees are visible here, although very
young seedlings of pine and birch have begun to sprout, almost hidden in the
messy clumps of grasses. Here and there a depression in the grass along with
clumps of deer droppings suggest that deer have bedded down in this area
recently, and the deer trails that have been worn to dirt hint at where they
have gone. To the east and south low hills rise up, impassable due to sheer,
crumbling dropoffs.
name: Shrubby Grassland by Low Hills

desc:
Low grasses dominate this area, suggesting that these fields were farmed until
relatively recently. The grasses are a mix of green blades from the current
season's growth and old, brown seed heads and stems from previous years; some of
these have bent and broken, giving the area a messy appearance. Some thin,
broadleaf tree seedlings rise up through the grass, young whips that have barely
begun to branch. No larger trees are present here, and no stumps are visible:
the tallest inhabitants of these fields are the tall bushes to the west that
make up an old hedgerow. The hedge is a tangled mess, clearly not maintained,
and tall weed stalks grow up through its base to stick out the sides and top.
Through the hedge a wide, paved road can barely be made out, while to the north
a dirt farm road is visible.
name: Hedge in the Low Grass

desc:
This area looks as if it were cleared recently, and not given much time to
regrow. No mature trees dot this landscape, and only a few young pine and birch
seedlings have started to grow, reaching just above the tall grasses that cover
the ground. Some low-growing brambles are tangled in the grasses, with older
dead grass stems poking through and wrapped around the thorny branches. Here and
there the grasses have been flattened and trampled down, old and new stems bent
or lying completely flat, suggesting that an animal has bedded down here
recently. To the west a long row of bushes blocks movement, an old hedgerow that
has become overgrown. It is irregular in height, the lower portions thinned out
and shaded by a twining vine that covers it.
name: Hedge in the Overgrown Fields

desc:
No mature trees grow here; the area is covered with thick brush instead. Tall
gorse bushes grow in tangled clumps, threaded through with lower-growing
brambles, creating large patches of thorny, restrictive barriers that are best
avoided. Trampled-down deer trails bypass these obstacles, thin ribbons that
follow the most open routes through the brush. To the west the tall brush is
bordered by a taller hedgerow, unkempt and messy in appearance, with young birch
trees poking through its dense branches as they reach for the sky. To the east
the ground rises up, turning into low, rolling hills, but the brush growing at
their base is too thick to pass through.
name: Hedge in the Brushy Fields

desc:
The ground here is hilly and uneven, forming low rolling hills. Despite the
relatively smooth undulations that mark this terrain, surprisingly steep cuts in
the ground are present, masked by the short heath and scrub bushes that grow
from the thin soil. These hidden drops and clefts require careful navigation to
avoid, and make riding through these hills too dangerous to attempt. Some of
these cuts are deeper than others, and a semi-connected line of narrow crevices
blocks travel to the east and south, where the hills continue.
name: Uneven, Hilly Heath

desc:
Varying types of grasses grow here, both a lower-growing type that has spread
evenly through the area and taller clumps that grow at regular intervals
throughout. Small, squat shrubs rise above the grasses, some growing in dense
clumps and others individually, adding height that is only topped by the stray
young pine tree that sticks up above all else. Various broad-leafed plants mix
in with the grasses, growing down close to the ground and up on tall hollow
stems. The ground underfoot is uneven and rolling, shallow depressions giving
way to low hummocks that lack vegetation and reveal bare, crumbling rock.
name: Sparse Shrubs in a Grassy Field

desc:
Here and there a mature tree rises from this field that is otherwise populated
only with stumps. Most of the felled trees have been removed, but an occasional
trunk still lies here, its branches stripped away and left piled nearby. Short
bushes mix with the grasses that currently dominate this area, their relatively
restrained growth suggesting that these woods were not cleared too long ago.
Young gorse bushes dot the landscape, their small size likely to change quickly
with the light available to them absent the trees that used to grow here.
name: Stumps before Steep Hills

desc:
This is the end of the dried-out brook that snakes through the plains and
occasional trees to the north. Beyond the old bank, rough fields lie both north
and south, while continued travel to the west is made impossible by too steep a
bank. Tree stumps dot the landscape, increasing in number towards the south.
name: End of a Dried-out Brook

desc:
A dried-out depression is the remnant of a stream that used to run through this
land. It meanders towards the south, through a natural ditch that runs
southwards. The surrounding fields are uneven but rich in plantlife. A stand of
old, large rowan trees can be seen growing to the east. Some tree stumps can be
seen in the surrounding fields.
name: Dried-out Brook

desc:
Too low and gentle to be called a hill, the land here is still elevated above
everything in sight except a range of hills far off in the distance to the
north-east. The grass and bushes here grow wild and free, undisturbed by man. A
long, gentle slope downwards to the north leads into a hollow, while the
elevated plains continue to the east. The Road to Fornost can be seen far to the
west, making its way from Bree to Fornost.
name: Elevated Plains

desc:
The surrounding fields dip into a hollow here, and at the bottom the ground
looks wet and soggy. The grass and bushes are mixed with reeds and other plants
that prefer more water-logged ground. Albeit all of this, there is no sign of
open water here, or a brook.
name: Quiet Hollow

desc:
The surrounding fields are rough and covered with natural ditches that run
southward. One of the most noticeable features in the area is a dried-out brook
bed that can be spotted coming from the east, and leading south, as it follows
one of the breaks in the land.
name: Dried-out Brook

desc:
A dried-out brook bed meanders here from the north before heading westward, as
it follows a slow decline in the land. Grasses have started to reclaim the brook
bed, covering nearly all the small pebbles and sand that the brook was unable to
move away, when water still flowed through it.
name: Dried-out Brook

desc:
The flat land sweeps away into the distance, wild and filled with bushes and
thick grasses. An enormous impenetrable wild rose-briar grows upon a steep
hillside to the northeast. The hillside is further covered by dense thickets as
it leads southward. A small stand of pine trees can be seen to the south.
name: Wild Plain

desc:
This is an old overgrown track lined with ancient linden trees, their trunks
covered in green moss. Past the trees on either side are small gardens of
pumpkins and gourds, and beyond these gardens are fields of beans. West, a
farmhouse guards this homestead with a steady view.
name: Old Farm Track

desc:
Rows of short green plants stretch over this field. One can only surmise that
these plants are turnips from the small pile of rotten turnips that have been
piled in a corner. North and east, stout fencing protects the field from larger
wildlife. Southwest, a large farmhouse stands proudly over this farm.
name: Turnip Field

desc:
Rows of apple and pear trees sprout their juicy-looking fruit. Some of the fruit
hangs low, ready to be picked. The odd grain or vegetable stock grows in the
broad gaps between the trees, apparently grown out of a stray seed from the
southern fields. West and north, the orchard is protected from deer by high
fences, interworked with hard, thorny lattice.
name: Orchard

desc:
This unkempt field contrasts with the other well tended fields of this farm.
Abandoned and broken carts, ploughs, saw horses, and yokes lie about. The rest
of the field grows various seeding crops. A large pile of vegetable matter is
piled high on the western edge near the fence, and has stained the wood dark
brown.
name: Unkempt Field

desc:
Wheat grows here in straight rows, occasionally blowing in the breeze as one
billowing mass. To the west, a stout fence marks the edge of the field. The
small footpaths between the crops are filled with roots and rocks, a tripping
hazard for the unwary traveller.
name: Field of Wheat

desc:
Oats grow up past a man's belly, bending and weaving in the breeze. The light
tan coloured field fills the air with a sweet grassy smell as the long leaves
rattle against each other. A stout white fence holds back the thick brush that
grows to the east.
name: Field of Tall Oats

desc:
A large field of vibrant barley is flanked by smaller fields of rye and oats. In
the southeast corner lays an old broken plough. South and east, the fields are
demarked by well kempt fences. Looking over the whole, is a grand farmhouse to
the northwest. Small paths run between the rows of crops.
name: Verdant Barley Fields

desc:
Rows of barley jam this well tended field. Small birds and insects dart in and
out of the field, feasting on the plant. A sturdy fence lines the west and
south. To the east and north more crops grow in neatly defined rows, footpaths
running between them.
name: Field of Barley

desc:
A meadow is full of grass, thick and healthy and it is evident why the barn
animals love to munch on this stuff. To the east is a gate guarding the crops.
South, the meadow continues unimpeded to a lush field, where the signs of cattle
trickle.
name: Thick Grass

desc:
Green and verdant meadow grass grows in abundance on this cattle field. To the
east, a tall fence marks bountiful fields of crops. The grassy field continues
far to the north and west, and the faint outline of thick bushes can be seen to
the south.
name: Lush Field

desc:
This field of sedge is trampled in paths by large animals. Obvious piles of dung
are scattered between the well munched grasses. The land becomes quite wet to
the south where a wide ditch filled with water and rushes would stop travel that
direction.
name: Animal Crossing

desc:
This lush field is high with clumped bulrush and the brown, poker seedheads of
reed mace, giving indication to swampy ground. South, the vegetation grows
upwards of eight feet, hiding a wide ditch. North, the field is demarked by a
stout, white fence.
name: Lush Field

desc:
This field of feral, green and purple grass gives way to unstable muck as it
drops down into a flooded ditch in the south. To the north, a well-maintained,
white-painted fence guards access to a farm. Over it, a distant barn can be seen
behind tops of neat rows of crops.
name: Field of Grass and Muck

desc:
This large meadow transitions from a field of sedge in the east to a rough bit
of gnarly weeds. It appears that the tough, tall weeds are advancing, taking
over the more animal-friendly fodder. To the south, rushes abound from a wide
ditch filled with water. North, a tall hedge full of green bushes stops travel.
name: Large Meadow

desc:
Thick, gnarly weeds cover this field. Their stems are tough and yield only when
under the weight of a full grown man. The field gently slopes into a muddy, wet
ditch that separates this field from a dirt road in the south. North, thick,
dull green hedges impede travel.
name: Gnarly Weeds

desc:
This meadow is filled with dead, thin tree trunks, surrounded by short, thick
grasses. Smells of sour muck and wet air give the land a deadened sound. Old
branches crunch underfoot and the air is filled with a heavy smell of decay.
name: Dead Tree Trunks

desc:
This lush field gives life to rich grasses. In the low-lying areas, butterbur
and moss grow on exposed rocks. Large, round hazel bushes are scattered about,
giving shade to withering ferns. To the south can be seen a derelict farm house,
while in the north, hedgerows block the way.
name: Rich Grasses

desc:
Purple headed grass billows soft and erect in this large field. The seed heads
look a sour grey when viewed up close and the grass leaves have soft hairs. The
field smells of fresh air, grass and dirt. Travel is unimpeded in all
directions.
name: Field of Fog Grass

desc:
A massive grazing field is enclosed by ancient brown hedges, blocking travel to
the south or west. Birds can be seen from time-to-time nipping at the hedge
berries. The field itself is well grazed, and piles of dung abound. Flies fly
lazily about, seeming to ignore the swallows that fly low, scooping them out of
the air.
name: Grazing Field

desc:
An extensive cow pasture is hemmed in on the north and west by a hedge of well
established bushes. The grass is trimmed low, and dung lays in piles drawing
numerous flies and the animals that feed on them. To the east and south, the
pasture continues.
name: Cow Pasture

desc:
A vast, lush field is overgrown with rangy, hard-stemmed chicory and leafy
goat's beard. Spotted on land are small blackthorn trees growing into tangles.
East and west, an overgrown, narrow track divides the field. A stone wall lines
the southern edge of the field. Tall, thorny bushes grow high above the wall,
blocking the view beyond.
name: Old Dirt Track

desc:
This grassy land stretches to the horizon, healthy and wild with succulent
sorrel, and hairy ribwort. A narrow track cuts through east and west, littered
with patches of black, hard shrubs, preventing straight lines of travel. The
northern edge of the land falls away into a waterlogged ditch filled completely
with tall grasses.
name: Grassy Land

desc:
An overgrown track runs east and west on this lush field of soft sweetbriar, and
clumped figwort. South, a crumbled line of stones mark what was once a slender
stone wall. A muddy, waterlogged ditch stands at the north of this field,
overgrown with bulrushes and soft, dark green agrimony.
name: Old Dirt Track

desc:
On a verdant field, barely seen between a morass of foxtail and cocksfoot, is
the likeness of a track giving no signs of humans passing this way for quite
some time. Thick hedges grow wildly to the south, almost swallowing that entire
side of the track. A watery, muddy ditch, filled with bulrushes and reeds,
blocks passage to the north.
name: Old Dirt Track

desc:
A morass of foxtail grass grows on this lush field, the bushy-tailed and upright
sward grows thick as it blows about in the breeze. Hidden in the grass is a dirt
track. Overgrown with grasses, it runs west to east and fades into this field. A
wide ditch to the north chokes the land with bushy clumps of meadow sweet,
blocking travel. South, rampant buckthorn grows in a thick hedge forming an
impassable wall.
name: Lush Field among a Dirt Path

desc:
This meadow is thick with sedge divided by lines of field maple. Going from east
to west, each line of maple seems to be growing out of a rut in the otherwise
untempered grass. A steep descent flows northwards, while to the south and
southwest, lush grasslands roll to the horizon.
name: Sedge and Field Maple Lined Meadow

desc:
Overgrown, busted fences mark fields that have grown back into a wild lea. The
few dejected outbuildings that still stand are choked with vines and weeds.
Inside the largest building the floor has collapsed in many places, revealing a
set of stairs leading down into a dark basement.
name: Overgrown Ruins

desc:
Dense, rippling grasslands stretch in all directions, green and silver, moving
with the wind as it wills. The ground is spongy, and gives underfoot, belying a
moisture that is evident with the vast number of gnats that whir in small, black
swarms.
name: Rippling Grassland

desc:
This field is lush and wild stretching to the horizon in all directions but
west, where a large, stony cliff stands tall, topped with green, tall bushes.
The land is thick with waist-high, dark red elder bushes interspersed with
clumps of fuzzy, green grasses. The air is abuzz with small gnats and bees, and
smells of sweet flowers.
name: Lush Field

desc:
The boggy gault has become too much for the grasses native to these meadows, and
they succumb to overbearing masses of bright green nettle. A pleasant, bitter,
salty fragrance fills the air. Travel is impeded to the east by thick muck
turning to a ditch and to the west an imposing escarpment looms.
name: Nettle-strewn Meadow

desc:
This meadow is overgrown with sedge, nettle and hazel bushes: a jumble of plants
fighting for life in the rich, wet soil. Lost in the rampant verdure are five
large boulders, weathered to the point of near disintegration. They sit far
apart, but seem to be about the same distance from each other. To the north and
west, passage is impossible due to the wild, thick plant life.
name: Five Large Boulders

desc:
A faint, spicy smell of hazel wafts along the breeze from hazel bushes looming
over the western rise. The land here is flat and lush, covered in grasses grown
of good moisture. North, east, and south the verdant view expands as far as the
eye can see.
name: Lea of a Hazel Rise

desc:
A substantial, low, exposed limestone crest gives life to hart's tongue, clover,
and spindle near the northern edge of this expanse of pink tinged sedge. The
ground rises a bit to the south where encroaching thorn bushes begin to lay
siege to the grass.
name: Limestone Crest

desc:
A field of waist-high grass sways about, green, tan, and purple. To the north,
the land increases to a plateau, becoming drier and harsh. This field drops in
the south, where it overlooks a watery, boggy land. To the east, the grasses
continue to the horizon.
name: Field of Swaying Grass

desc:
Hazel bushes grow in patches throughout this high land, buffeted about by a hard
wind. Grasses and smaller shrubs cover the rest of the plateau, grabbing onto
the hard, dry soil. The air is full of dust and the smell of earth is strong.
name: High Land

desc:
Tall grasses bob their heavy heads, nodding to large, green bushes along this
plateau. The bushes grow in groups, leafy, and rise from six to twenty feet.
Small white moths fly about the bushes, and contrast with the dark green leaves.
To the east, the plateau gives way to an unexpected cliff.
name: Bushy Plateau

desc:
Tall, dark green bushes sprout over this vast plateau. Standing upwards of
eighteen feet, they grow in clumps at low, rocky areas. Their glossy and bumpy
branches have grown thick into each other and a sweet fragrance fills the air.
Small, white moths flutter about and underfoot, small round nuts crunch easily.
In the higher land, brown grass grows short and defiant. To the east, the
plateau drops off, providing no footing.
name: Dark Green Bushes

desc:
These small hills are dotted by numerous thorny hawthorn bushes: silver and grey
at a distance, green and red up close. The earth is dense and covered in short
grasses and dandelions. In the north and east, the thorny bushes become more
sparse, opening out to grassy fields. In the south the bushes crowd the hills,
making travel more difficult.
name: Rolling Hills of Scattered Thornbushes

desc:
Lush, green grass covers the ground of a vast, open meadow. Scattered here and
there are small dirt mounds and sunken burrows. A thin, sparse tree line creates
a natural border to the south and west, hindering travel in those directions.
The vastness of the meadow expands to the north and east.
name: Green Meadow

desc:
A small watering hole is nestled in a gradual depression in an expansive, green
and purple bog. Lilies reach up from the clear depths to dot the surface of the
standing water, reaching the black surface with small green circles and yellow
flowers. The ground is boggy, with a slightly rotten odour, and drags down the
heavy of foot.
name: Small Watering Hole

desc:
A dense grove of willow trees surrounds the path. Narrow branches hang low
overhead with ghost-like moss hanging in small strips. The walkway's surface is
unusually well groomed, with clover patches spreading beyond the trail's
serpentine path through the surrounding hills.
name: Strange Path

desc:
A small path, slightly covered with leaves and old twigs, continues to the north
and east; bordered by tall bushes and trees with thin trunks and branches. To
the east the vegetation becomes thinner until it fades away into the low grass
of a plain.
name: Strange Path

desc:
A grassy hill emerges from the surroundings, the sides of which are slippery
from densely clustered vines with smooth leaves. The ground appears trampled,
pounding the vines into a solid surface. To the east and north a trail may be
seen avoiding the hill.
name: Grassy Hillside

desc:
This path has been nearly overtaken by an old, thorny bush that has been
encroaching upon it for decades. It seems however, that at some point in the
past someone used a sturdy rope to bind its branches from completely blocking
the way of those travelling along the path.
name: Strange Path

desc:
Between the low and high bushes this path continues without any discernible
pattern. It turns here and there, and back and forth leading travellers
sometimes around bushes and other times through them. Short grass grows on the
soft earth and dry sticks lie scattered around.
name: Strange Path

desc:
Rich soil must be the reason this plain is covered in such vibrantly healthy
grass. Many different varieties of grass grow here. In some spots, the stalks
are high and slender, while at other points there are blades of grass with thick
stems and broad leaves.
name: Multi-coloured Grasslands

desc:
Short grass covers these plains which stretch out in all directions. The break
in the carpet of grass is offered by a few lost bushes that emerge from the flat
ground. In some spots, the droppings of rabbits and other small animal tracks
indicate the plain is not as lifeless as it may look.
name: Windy Plains

desc:
On this plain grows mostly short, unhealthy grass. Even the occasional bush
grows very low, their branches being brown and covered in thorns. Yet, at
several spots grow rich clusters of plants. It looks as if the plain would make
up at these spots for all the other shortcomings. Rich grass, flowers with thick
stems and high bushes seem to grow out of the meagre soil for no apparent
reason.
name: Dusty Meadowland

desc:
Below the short grass of the plain many flat stones are hidden. The soil itself
seems to consist only of hardened earth and stone, while only few animals have
left their tracks in the poor vegetation. The plain continues until it meets the
horizon.
name: Stony Plains

desc:
The ground is covered in a short carpet of green grass, interspersed with clover
and dandelion. The air is filled with sweet smells, and near a low, lush shallow
grow a bunch of graceful willows. As far as the eye can see plains extend, with
a slight incline eastward giving a lingering view.
name: Graceful Willows

desc:
A series of rolling crests in the land stretch from north to south. Ascending to
the west, thorny shrubs infest a high point in the plains where the grass grows
with much less vigour. Flowers, mostly forget-me-nots, line the rolling plains
as they spread out to the north and east.
name: Rolling Fields

desc:
Rodent colonies have claimed the area as their own. The natural landscape has
been completely altered with burrows dotting the area with small grassy mounds
next to them. The grass has been kept short from their constant foraging.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
A huge, solitary tree, spans high into the sky above. Its dense branches and
leaves create a huge canopy directly beneath it. Several yards away, a tall,
fractured rock stands stricken by lightning. Blackened by fire, this rock must
have been scorched a while ago as the grass around it grows tall and healthy. To
the south, the grass grows shorter as it rises to an area of thorny shrubs,
while in all other directions, the tall grasses wave and bow on rolling plains.
name: Base of a Huge Tree

desc:
A vast expanse of heaving grasses flows in all directions. A few trees and
boulders are all that prevent the wind from flowing as it wills, yet even the
boulders appear worn smooth by the wind, showing few protrusions on their flat
heads. With every step on the lush grassland, the ground seems to spring back
up, fresh and light.
name: Heaving Grassland

desc:
A sea of tall grass descends from the northeast in a series of gentle drops
dividing flatter areas as if the wind has carved a broad staircase out of the
swelling plain, creating a crescent dip in the grasslands before rising up again
in the southwest. The skyline in the north and west bobs up and down as gently
as the breeze that flows through this area.
name: Crescent-shaped Dip in the Plains

desc:
The billowing grasslands rise in a series of gradual steps to a swell. Before
the apex of the swell can be reached, a line of crude bedrock stands guard over
the ascending land. Stripped of its soil from the relentless wind, the bedrock
offers no foothold to ascend, though they stand just a few feet above the head
of a tall man. Away from the rocks, to the south and west, the ground dips to a
low point in the plains in a crescent-shaped line.
name: Billowing Grassland

desc:
This field sprouts random oats and wheat among the vigorous sedge. The forgotten
land has fallen back to nature, and seems the better for it. North, west, and
south the land fades to the horizon. To the east, a rampant wall of shiny barked
buckthorn blocks the way.
name: Vigorous Sedge

desc:
The air is extremely musty, making breathing difficult. Moisture drips down the
old, stone walls and collects in a puddle in the ground. The dank, stale water
overflows into a dark hole in the corner of the cellar, creating a distant
dripping sound from down below. A rotten pile of burlap bags lays heaped in one
corner, and in another is a mound of broken clay jars.
name: Musty Root Cellar

desc:
Waist-high grass bobs and sways under the weight of the flowing wind. With every
breeze, the grass dips low enough to reveal colourful flowers that add a vibrant
hue to the deep green of the grasses. The grassland declines gently northwards
where, at an abrupt step, it lifts above the surrounding fields until it is
higher than a tall man.
name: Thick Grassy Field

desc:
The ground here is covered in clover and thistle, spreading green and low, and
small stands of field maple attempt to fight for life against the teeming
fescue. Grasshoppers and flying ants bounce around, trying to avoid the low
flying swallows that swoop about.
name: Clover and Thistle

desc:
Sweeping fields of low grass extend in all directions, growing gradually taller
to the north and west. The landscape is almost entirely green, with a few wild
flowers lending their vibrant hues to the otherwise uninterrupted flows of
verdant flora. The turf is moist, and springs beneath every step. Occasional
rustling sounds can be heard from small game scampering through the grass.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
Scattered weeds grow among wispy grasses. Occasionally, a bee can be seen
bouncing between the few flowering plants of this field. Sloping upwards in the
east, the ground crests into a ridge, which lines the eastern and northeastern
horizon. In the west, the ground levels out to an expanse of lush fields, while
in the south the land rolls forming foothills.
name: Thin Grasses

desc:
Rolling hills are covered with waving grass bending in the wind, dancing
intricately with each passing gust. The grass is so thick that nothing beneath
the green waves is visible, only the contour of the land. Lone trees dot the
surroundings, their trunks standing out among the grassy waves like rough, brown
pillars.
name: Grass-covered Hills

desc:
Only poor vegetation is growing here. Everything that grows leaves seems to
quickly become eaten by animals, as many ripped stems indicate. There are some
holes in the crumbled earth, probably having been dug by some small varmint, but
there is no sign any animal has lived in them for quite a while.
name: Barren Grasslands

desc:
A forest fire has recently blazed through the area, evidence of its heat has
scarred the landscape. Burned trunks are all that remain of trees, some hollowed
out from the fire. Branches that have burned to a crisp and ash mounds litter
the ground.
name: Burned Hills

desc:
A lone, burned tree is the only thing that catches the eye on this plain. Apart
from the tree, only short, brown grass and low bushes can be seen. The remains
of the tree are blackened and split in two, and it looks as if lightning had
