struck and ignited it. Since there is no other sign of an ignition source and
ivy has started to grow on the dead trunk, quite some time must have passed.
name: Burned Prairie

desc:
This plain seems to be very fertile, the soil is soft and the grass grows thick.
Yet, there is not much to see since most of the vegetation has been eaten by
animals and has not yet grown back. The traces which have not been removed by
wind or rain, and the echoes of distant bleating, indicate a herd of sheep has
spent some time here and moved on, as they finished eating.
name: Plains

desc:
A small group of boulders is the only change between the low grass and small,
widespread bushes on this plain. The highest of these stones stands three feet
above the plain, and it can not be told what brought and piled these stones
here. The view continues in any direction until it meets the horizon, only to
the south grows more vegetation and the outlines of trees stand out.
name: Rocky Prairie

desc:
High, slightly yellow grass covers the hills and moves in the wind like waves in
the ocean. From time to time, paths created by other travellers or animals cross
each other. The hills continue to gently roll in most directions, and tree lines
block passage to the north and south.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Winding around the hill, a path makes its way up and down the hill. Sparse
vegetation covers the path while a few small trees grow on its sides. Sometimes,
patches of pebbles lie on the path, making the ground a bit harder to travel on.
name: Small Path through Some Hills

desc:
A few bushes peek out of the grass which lines a small path leading to the
hilltop. A few flowers grow on the sides of the path in between the other sparse
vegetation. Most parts of the hill itself are covered with medium-high grass.
name: Small Path through Some Hills

desc:
An unfrequented path winds itself through the rolling hills that cascade the
horizon. Tall golden prairie grass skirts the beaten path to expose an
accessible foot trail. Only sparse vegetation and trees with light-brown bark
can be seen in the surrounding landscape.
name: Small Path

desc:
Enormous trees loom over a paved road with many smaller bushes and plants as it
leads through the surrounding landscape. Groups of young oak trees have recently
grown among the fading patches of grass revealing a much smaller, less-travelled
path towards the north.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The grassy field is covered with patches of small red flowers. Old flower beds
are scattered throughout the field, some of them still contained within their
original flower beds. Others have escaped their confinement and now grow wild
throughout the grass.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
A small, dark pond with steep, slippery banks lies here, barren from any type of
tracks. On the water's surface float lazily a few lost leaves, slowly sinking
into the darkness. In the murky water, dark green plants barely grow by the
muddy shore, covered in slimy algae. Cattails grow in patches around the pond,
in the thick brown mud.
name: Small, Dark Pond

desc:
Only a few scattered bushes and tall grasses grow here. A few trees with thin
branches have taken root but most of them are pretty low. Only very recently,
some travellers must have taken a rest on this plain, since the remains of two
small campfires can be spotted.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
Various rubble lie strewn about the field, consisting of various sizes of broken
cobblestones. Tall weeds grow between the rocks and in the cracks of the stones,
seemingly to cause some to splinter asunder. Large piles form a wall against
ancient barkless trees to the east.
name: Rocky Field

desc:
An open field lies between hills to the west and a grove of oak trees to the
east. Patches of bushy vegetation dot the area and a small ditch flows from the
hills and cuts into the ground along the roots of the trees, which seem to swell
from the water and grow.
name: Open Field

desc:
Soft grasses can be seen covering a nearly flat field, stretching until the
horizon meets the sky. In some spots, an animal has dug a hole in the ground and
ripped up the turf. In these places the brown and fertile soil can be seen.
name: Open Field

desc:
At the crest of the small hill grows an extremely old, twisted tree that is
surrounded by tall, dead grass. A few younger shrubs can be seen down the
hillside. The base of the twisted tree is so wide it must have seen hundreds of
winters. A second look reveals a large hole leading down beneath the roots of
the elderly tree with a mysterious and damp odour.
name: Small Hill

desc:
Grassy, lush hills transform into dry and rocky highlands to the north, the
greenery turning more brown and silver as the land becomes drier. Thorny weeds
and low shrubs provide stable handholds on the steeper drops, and the soil skids
out from under foot, loose and brown.
name: Lush Hills

desc:
Wildflowers bloom in patches along the grassy hillsides. Low hanging blue, bell
shaped flowers bounce lazily in the wind, heavy with nectar. The air is laden
with pollen, almost creating a yellow haze which settles into a dense carpet of
fine dust on the ground.
name: Grass-covered Hills

desc:
Spongy green moss has formed a dense blanket covering the ground. The ground is
soft and saturated with water, allowing the lichen to thrive. The moss has
covered most of the vegetation in the area, and branches of bushes hang low with
the moss strands dangling from them.
name: Mossy Field

desc:
Patches of grass interspersed with tufts of bare bedrock extend down from the
ridge in the west and to the slope in the north. As the land flattens out, rocks
gradually become less frequent and waist-high grass takes over. To the east and
south, the field drops precipitously, providing a good view of the road to
Fornost below.
name: Rocky Field

desc:
Dark dirt rankles with ever increasing stones, rocks and boulders as the land
rises westward. Sedge, cocksfoot, and foxtail clamp onto any exposed earth.
North, the land is blocked by a crumbling stone wall and to the west travel
becomes arduous, the land rising to a ridge.
name: Sloping, Rocky Field

desc:
This high, rolling land is dry and rocky, and grasses are short and stiff. Some
hardy brushes grow silver and dusty, their leaves long and thin. The descent to
either side is precarious, with only a few straggly weeds holding together the
rocky soil. Far in the east, the lane can be seen winding its way around hills.
name: High Rolling Land

desc:
Luscious currant and gooseberry bushes grow wild on this field, giving evidence
to the crops that were once cultivated. Tall grasses often stick their heads up
over the short, green bushes, trespassing in abrupt clumps. On the northern edge
of the field, a crumbling fence lies, now more a long mound of stones. Far to
the east, a rusted gate stands crumbling amidst a mossy stone wall.
name: Currant and Gooseberry Bushes

desc:
Long, wavy strands of grass flutter over the tops of wildly growing crops. The
heads of oat and barley stalks are picked at by a multitude of black birds and
the air is thick with small swarms of flies, bees, and wasps. A stone wall
stretches along the western side of this field. Unlike the north wall that is
just crumbled remains, this wall has been plugged with dirt and rocks, creating
a patchwork of grey and brown.
name: Wild Crops

desc:
Long, wavy strands of grass flutter over the tops of an abandoned field of rye.
A barren, rocky ridge rises southwards, casting a long, pointed shadow over this
field. A decrepit stone wall appears to have been built over what was once the
tail end of the ridge, but has now been reduced to little more than a bump in
the field. To the east and north the field continues, while in the west the land
becomes loose and rocky.
name: Abandoned Field of Rye

desc:
Patches of wild oats push their stocks above a sea of tall grass. A few other
food-bearing crops grow haphazardly throughout the field, as if this were a farm
tended only by the animals that graze on it. A crumbling stone wall lines the
land south and east.
name: Field of Wild Oats

desc:
Tough, purple grasses and short, silver bushes weave and wind their way through
scattered rocks. In places, rocks fall into treacherous, deep craters. The
southern part of this field is so filled with the hazards that there is no clear
path through them.
name: Scattered Rocks

desc:
The surrounding field swells up here to a rise of hard soil and loose shrubbery.
The view of the fields in the west and south is acute as the land drops at a
short, dirt cliff. Passage to the north is blocked by thorny buckthorn trees,
tight and thick.
name: Shrub-filled Field

desc:
In a great, rolling field, a small grove of trees has been planted with some
foresight. On the outside, rowans grow smooth and silver, their red berries
abounding. On the inside a great yew stands, defiant. Nary a sound is heard in
the middle of the grove, caused by the yew needles underfoot.
name: Secluded Grove

desc:
This land is wild with large clumps of grasses interspersed in the low ground
with shiny barked buckthorn. To the north, west, and south, the buckthorn grows
wild and thick, forming prickly, impassable hedges. In the air, birds fly
lazily, drugged by the weak poison of the trees' berries.
name: Large Clumps of Grasses

desc:
An ancient, ruined estate of great lawns and paths of gravel sprawl here, dry
and fierce, thick with thistle. Thorny cherry trees stick their hard, barbed
branches on the edges of the land in deliberate rows. In the east, the ruins of
a stately farmhouse are being swallowed by thorns and brambles which climb to
the sky like pointy, grasping fingers.
name: Ruined Estate

desc:
A large road leads through a landscape of grass and green. Thousands of
merchants' carriages have turned the soil into a hard road. Many low bushes have
grown all around spotting the grassland with dots of different shades of green.
name: Greenway

desc:
Large, leafy weeds have overrun an old field. Old furrows have begun to fall
into disarray, and it is not easy to tell from under the weeds where one ends
and others begin. Small patches of rotten pumpkins and squash dot the field in
areas where the weeds have not taken complete control.
name: Abandoned Field

desc:
Vines grow here in many ordered rows. Someone must have once worked very hard
since countless stakes have been put into the ground to give the vines the
support they need to grow straight upwards. Tall weeds now fill the spaces
between the rows of vines.
name: Overgrown Vineyard

desc:
While the brushwood has been removed some time ago it has started to grow back
between the trees. The ground in this light forest is covered with soft moss and
clusters of higher grass, with a noticeable, well-worn path cutting east and
west. All around, small, fallen sticks and loppings can be spotted.
name: Trail through a Light Forest

desc:
Most of this area is lightly forested with low, wide trees whose canopy allows
ample sunlight to reach the forest floor on clear days. A wide trail curves
between the trees, heading westward. To the east, large piles of neatly stacked
brushwood block any advance in that direction. In the southeastern distance, the
trail continues, visible between the widely spaced tree trunks.
name: Trail through a Light Forest

desc:
A deep, hollow way has been carved into the landscape and bends north into the
Chetwood forest from the village of Archet to the east. Tracks of horses and
wagon wheels are engraved deep into the trail, which is muddy and rough. The
smell of damp and rotten wood fills the air.
name: Hollow Way

desc:
A well-travelled trail connects the southern villages of Staddle and Combe with
the more distant town of Archet, nestled deep in the Chetwood forest. The wheels
of overladen wagons have carved deep ruts into the otherwise soft forest floor.
Some footprints and wheel tracks lead north where the trail meanders deeper into
the wood.
name: Intersection in a Village Trail

desc:
Cleared of stray branches, leaf litter, and saplings, a rough trail winds its
way through the forest. Occasionally, low bramble bushes impinge at the trail's
edges and, in other places, planks of roughly hewn lumber have been laid across
particularly damp stretches of the trail.
name: Winding Forest Trail

desc:
The forest trail is not well-maintained in this area, and it is often obscured
by ferns, bramble, and eager saplings. Fortunately, the trees are spaced apart
widely enough that finding the trail again is simply a matter of looking a bit
farther ahead.
name: Overgrown Forest Trail

desc:
Oak, aspen, and giant fir trees span out in every direction, and the forest
floor at the edges of the trail is crowded with ferns and brushwood. Off the
trail, finding a way through the dense undergrowth would be a tiresome and
tedious feat. No direct sunlight penetrates this place; instead, when the sun is
directly overhead, an ambient, golden glow suffuses the dense forest.
name: Trail through Dense Forest

desc:
Birch trees cover the hillside, their leafy branches blotting out the sky. A
lone hollow tree stands at the top in a grassy field. The trunk has been scarred
with small holes, filled with nuts of all sorts. Rough stones of all shapes and
sizes dot the ground.
name: Forested Hills

desc:
The ground slants ever so slightly downward to the east as it completes its
descent from the western hilltop. Expansive oaks stretch their long branches
high and wide overhead, shading much of the landscape. Underfoot, tiny caches of
acorns are piled up on the leaf litter.
name: Lightly-wooded Slope

desc:
The forest is crowded here, dominated by oaks above and alder buckthorn below.
The trees clump together, blocking any path to the east or south. In the places
where the great oaks have yet to claim their territory, dogwood trees take their
humble stand, stretching their limbs low and wide.
name: Crowded Forest

desc:
Mature trees grow in this area, but they are spaced far apart. Between them
younger trees rise up, too small to cast significant shade, and the light
reaching the ground has given rise to a dense brush cover that makes travel
through the area difficult. The occasional fallen tree lies tangled within this
brush, already covered by climbing vines, brambles, and tall, arching herbaceous
weed stalks; these fallen trunks are largely hidden from view until stumbled
upon. The easiest movement through this thin woodland is following the deer
trails that wind between the trees, these narrow gaps through the brush still
blocked in places by the stray branch reaching across.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
Tall, messy grasses dominate these fields, growing in clumps so close together
that they provide continuous coverage of the soil. Many of the clumps are no
longer standing straight up, their new green growth mixed with old, brown stems
from past years that have bent and broken and pushed the entire clump to lean to
one side or another. Here and there short scrubby pine trees have begun to grow,
still small enough to be mostly hidden by the surrounding grass, and young gorse
bushes face the same struggle to compete for light. To the east a tree line
begins, the tangled brush and mature trunks forming an impassable barrier, while
to the north a line of tangled shrubs mixed with briars prevents passage onto
what appears to be a wide, paved road.
name: Overgrown Field

desc:
Tall, messy clumps of grasses cover the ground here. Many of the clumps are
topped with broken stems still bearing previous years' flower heads, the old
brown growth contrasting with this year's green. Closer to the ground, more
remnants of old growth are visible, loose curving brown blades and broken hollow
stems collecting in a tangled mess that has yet to decay into the soil. Short
bushes dot the fields at intervals, rising just far enough above the dense
grasses to be able to compete for light. To the west hills rise up, low but
steep enough to prevent movement. Their eastern face, visible from here, has
eroded to expose bare rock. To the south is a light forest, and the border is a
barrier of mature trees interspersed with tangled brush, dense enough to prevent
movement in that direction.
name: Scrubby Fields

desc:
Tall, messy grasses dominate these fields, growing in clumps with a lower,
finer-textured grass spreading in the spaces between. Dead stems and old flower
heads are mixed in with the grass clumps, the growth of past years that has not
yet broken down into the soil. Low bushes grow here in groups, with the
outermost bushes of each group struggling to gain height over the dense grasses
that grow through, next to and over them. To the west, hills rise up, not tall
but steep enough to block movement. Erosion channels cut through these steep
slopes, channelling water from rains down into the fields here; at the
termination of each, the grasses extending beyond seem to grow taller and
denser. To the north a wide, paved road can be seen.
name: Scrubby Fields

desc:
Rugged hills of rock and dirt protrude from the larger hills in the south.
Reaching beyond the northwest corner of the forest, the hills jab into the road
to Fornost as they curve between the forest and a stone wall. The elongated
hills alternate between crude bumps and cutting valleys just wide enough to lose
one's footing into. Near where the hills meet the road, on the northeastern
side, they level out into a relatively even descent.
name: Rugged, Rocky Hills

desc:
Lightly wooded hills divide Chetwood in the east from the road to Fornost in the
west. Not quite rising to the heights of the trees at the base of the hills,
they offer little view other than a passing glimpse of those travelling the road
directly to the west. Beyond the road, rising above the tree canopy, a barren
ridge forms a straight line across the horizon.
name: Lightly Wooded Hills

desc:
Some kind of animal, perhaps deer, seems to have nibbled on the barks of the
trees in this light forest. These marks can be found on many trees at the height
of maybe two to three feet from the ground. To the west a large pile of cut
brushwood has been overgrown by grass.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Many tracks left by heavy carts can be seen in the soft ground. In many places,
stumps of fresh-cut brushwood stick out of the grass. Not long ago, the
brushwood must have been removed. Since the light trees have not grown close
together the eye can see a relatively long distance in this forest.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Dust devils blow about the plains, obscuring the view with dust and debris.
Patches of grass try to hold the ground together in patches. Where the
vegetation is missing, the wind has eroded the ground and laid bare hard, red
clay.
name: Dusty Plains

desc:
Mature trees grow here, but they are spaced far enough from each other that
plenty of light makes it to the ground below. Younger trees, mostly oak, are
starting to grow in these gaps, rising up above the tall grasses and shrubs that
dominate the understory. The ground underfoot is soft, with a layer of dead
leaves from the previous season mixing with the grasses and crumbling into the
soil. Some deer trails run through these light woods, winding around the mature
trees and marked at intervals by piles of deer droppings.
name: Spaced-out Trees

desc:
This mature forest is composed primarily of oak trees, with some rowan and birch
mixed in. A few scraggly pine trees remain, although they appear to be dying out
due to the lack of light beneath the canopy above: their lower branches are bare
and broken, and only their tops still have green needles. The undergrowth on the
forest floor is sparse here, due to the lack of light, limited to small
herbaceous plants with large leaves and some short ferns that are confined to
shallow depressions where the soil must retain water after a rain. To the north
and east the forest thins out, the trees spaced far enough apart that light is
able to reach through the canopy to the ground below.
name: Mature Oak Forest

desc:
This mature deciduous forest is composed of oak and rowan trees, growing closely
enough together to form a solid canopy overhead that blocks sunlight from
reaching the ground below. Here and there the canopy thins slightly from where a
tree has fallen in the past, but the surrounding trees have already filled in
enough of the gap that no significant light makes it through. Old tree trunks
lay on the forest floor, already starting to decompose, the bark sloughing off
and lying in piles that are mixed with leaf debris from previous seasons. These
trunks are drilled full of small holes, and lined with shelves of small,
crinkly-edged fungi.
name: Dense Oak Forest

desc:
The trees in this forest are spaced far enough apart that plenty of light makes
it through to the understory. Many of the trees are pines, short and stubby and
being overtaken by taller deciduous trees. Most of these pines are bare up to
their tops, where a small green canopy of needles remains; only those that are
far away from taller trees are still green all the way down. Small bushes and
tall grasses grow between the trees, taking advantage of the light coming
through the sparse canopy above. The forest grows more dense to the south, where
the larger deciduous trees have taken over, growing closely enough together to
form a solid canopy.
name: Spaced-out Trees

desc:
At the edge of this forest, widely-spaced, short pine trees grow in abundance.
They are interspersed with birch, and in some places clumps of birch are
starting to rise over and shade out some of the pines. Plenty of light is able
to reach the ground through this discontinuous canopy, allowing thick brush to
grow between the trees. The brush is tall and tangled in some places, but mostly
it grows low enough to allow easy movement. Gorse bushes are mixed with
lower-growing heath and the occasional blueberry bush, many growing closely
enough together that they will be competing for light in a few years' time. The
forest grows more dense to the southeast, with the pines gradually replaced by
taller deciduous trees.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
The trees here at the edge of the forest are sparse, some growing in clumps but
most spaced widely apart. They are mostly birch and pine, and rise up above
tall, messy grasses that are choked with dead stems and old flower heads. Mixed
with the grasses, and growing around the trees in the areas with the most light
available, are tall gorse bushes, their branches tangled and scruffy. Briars are
threaded through these with long, thorny stems, making travel through the brush
hard, forcing travel to deer trails that wind around the trees.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
This area was once grassy, but shrubs are starting to overtake the grasses,
shading and thinning them out. Gorse bushes mix with lower growing heath to form
a shrubland of various heights, clumps merging into dense, wide thickets that
extend to cover a significant amount of ground. Here and there flattened
sections of brush show where deer have bed down, and clumps of deer droppings
mark indistinct deer trails through the grassier areas of the brush. Some young
pine and birch trees have begun to grow through the shorter areas of shrubs,
branching after they have topped their neighbours, and beginning to shade out
anything beneath.
name: Shrubland by a Forest

desc:
The trees at this forest edge are spaced fairly far apart, allowing light to
filter through to the ground. Many of the trees are mature, but there are also a
significant number of saplings and young trees beginning to grow in the gaps.
Most of the trees here are pine and birch, although some younger oak trees
appear to be getting established as well. The understory is brushy and dense,
with briars and gorse bushes growing in the light filtering through the broken
canopy above, and to the east and south this brush grows densely enough to block
movement farther into the forest that lies in those directions.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
This area is covered with low-growing grasses, both a spreading type and one
that has formed clumps topped with old, brown flower stems from the previous
season's growth. Some small bushes grow through the thick grasses, heath and
young gorse, individually and in clumps dense enough to outcompete the
surrounding vegetation. There are a few small pine trees locked in a similar
competition: they are all small and squat, their lowest, outer branches grown
through with tall grass blades, and their tops poking above as sprays of green
needles. The ground underfoot is uneven and rolling, dipping and rising with
regularity until it joins the hills in the distance to the northeast.
name: Rolling Grassland

desc:
This area is covered by low vegetation, a mix of various low-growing grasses and
short shrubs that reach no higher than knee-high on a man. In some areas the
shrubs appear thin and spindly, with grasses growing right up through them, but
in other spots the shrubs have formed dense clumps that have managed to
outcompete the other vegetation. Here and there short, scrubby pine trees are
just getting started, forming small mounds that peek above the dense grass. To
the east and north the ground rises up and low hills begin, although long
shelves of crumbling rock cut across the hills where the slopes become steeper,
making passage too difficult and dangerous in those directions.
name: Short Shrubland by Low Hills

desc:
Tall oat grass covers this area, although it is being outcompeted by shrubs,
mostly gorse bushes. These are growing in clumps and groups, forming large and
tall thickets of dense, tangled brush, with relatively open grassy areas
between. Some short, spindly pine tree seedlings are starting to grow in the
grassy areas, their tufts of long, soft needles just barely reaching above the
tops of the grass clumps, but otherwise this area seems to be devoid of trees.
To the north and west long thickets of gorse bushes merge into solid rows of
shrubs that prevent passage.
name: Tall Shrubland

desc:
Low growing bent grass and heath are being displaced here by taller shrubs that
grow in tangled, messy clumps. Briars and gorse bushes mix to create tall, wide
thickets that cover a significant amount of ground, broken up by grassy spots
full of old weed stalks. The ground underfoot is uneven, with large hummocks
rising up before sinking into shallow depressions; many of these depressions are
filled with shorter, greener grasses and sparse brush, suggesting that the soil
stays waterlogged after a rain for longer than the rest of the vegetation in
this area can tolerate. Here and there a small birch tree rises through a
thicket, spreading its branches above as it reaches for the sky.
name: Briars in the Shrubland

desc:
Clumps of tall grasses are being displaced here by tall shrubs, mostly gorse
bushes and brambles. These shrubs rise up to form tall, thorny thickets that are
easier to move around than through. Lower-growing heath and broadleaf weeds grow
in the areas between these thickets, competing for light and leaving behind the
shaded, skeletal remains of the losers. The brush is thick enough here that the
only direction not blocked by a maze of tangled thickets is east, where enough
lower-growing areas connect to be followed as a trail.
name: Tall Shrubland

desc:
This small cave has been hollowed out of the earth beneath a large hummock in
the brushland. The ceiling seems to be held together by a dense mat of roots
from the vegetation growing above, and in some places these roots trail down,
adorned with small clods of dirt that dangle in mid-air. Clumps of fur gather in
small piles in corners of this burrow, and the loose dirt floor is littered with
small rocks and what appears to be the occasional bone of a small animal. At the
entrance to the cave debris is piled up by the opening: dead leaves, plant
stalks, and shreds of bark.
name: Small Hollow under the Brush

desc:
This area is open and grassy, with the occasional mature tree that casts minimal
shade. Low-growing grasses and the occasional heath or gorse bush cover the
ground, which is uneven and rolling, with broad depressions and low hummocks.
Here and there the vegetation thins out, the soil giving way to bare, exposed
rock, which is tan and crumbling. To the north the ground becomes hilly, but
near-vertical cuts along the sides of the steeper hills form enough of a barrier
to prevent movement in that direction. To the south is a dense forest, its
border overgrown with brush and impassable.
name: Rolling Grassland by Low Hills

desc:
An occasional mature tree rises above this grassland, but the vegetation here
consists primarily of grasses and the occasional low bush. The ground is uneven,
with many low hummocks and shallow depressions; on some of the hummocks rock
peeks through the thin soil. To the south a forest rises above the plains, its
edge dense with brush to the point of being impassable. To the north a series of
crumbling, exposed rock ledges blocks the way to the hills that rise steeply
above.
name: Uneven Grassland by Low Hills

desc:
A few mature trees dot these hills, but otherwise this area is open, the ground
underfoot uneven and rolling. Low grasses cover the ground, mixed with heath and
the occasional clump of gorse bushes. The grass in the low spots seems to be
thicker and taller, suggesting that this area is low-lying, with water
collecting in the depressions after a rain. To the south a dense forest can be
seen, while to the north the ground rises sharply into a range of hills. Passage
into the hills to the north is blocked by ledges of crumbling rock, too unstable
to traverse or climb.
name: Uneven Ground by Low Hills

desc:
Brushy, red-twigged dogwood covers this morass with rampant, white-flowered
branches. The shrubs only grow up to the waist of a man, but are so thick in the
moist soil as to cause difficult travel. Grey, white-flecked alder trees fight
for life in the wetter dips, their shiny green, leathery leaves set off against
the red of the lower plants. Meandering through the brush is a seldom used
trail, running roughly northeast to southwest.
name: Brushy Morass

desc:
The narrow path bends as it makes its way through the bleak land, its path
leading to the north and east. Some rocky hills prevent travel to the south
while the rest of the ground is covered in heath. Few grasses, shrubs, and trees
are able to take root here in the poor soil.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
The narrow path continues straight north and south here, through the wild
meadows to the east and west. A hill rises in the distance to the north.
Otherwise, the land is flat and open, surrounded by waves of tall grasses and
forbs flowing around islands of bushes.
name: Narrow Track

desc:
A small stand of tall, straight pines grows to the west, at the base of a tall
ridge. A wild plain, filled with grazed grasses, stretches out in all other
directions. Only the occasional tree or bush breaks the monotony of the
landscape, with trees becoming more rare as the land moves south.
name: Stand of Trees on a Wild Plain

desc:
A bleak land stretches out far into the distance before terminating at some
rocky hills to the south. Some grasses do manage to take root here, but most of
the ground is covered in heath. The occasional stunted tree and shrub can be
seen taking root where the soil was rich enough.
name: Sparse Heathland

desc:
A valley of heath stretches here, nested between some rocky hills to the south,
and a ridge topped with rowan trees to the north. A large number of shrubs grow
in abundance here, finding pockets of soil rich enough to sustain them. Some
meagre clumps of grass have also taken root along with an occasional tree.
name: Sparse Heathland

desc:
A slight ridge inclines here out of the western fields, to a high point where a
cluster of rowan trees has grown at its rocky top. They stand out among the
surrounding fields, and a dried-out brook bed can be spotted towards the
northwest. A valley stretches out towards the east, dotted with shrubs.
name: Ridge with a Cluster of Rowan Trees

desc:
The ground drops down steeply here into a deep hollow. The air is permeated by a
thick, damp mist, and an earthy smell of soggy leaves and toadstools is a
constant presence here. The haphazard way in which this hollow was dug seems to
indicate it was created by animals.
name: Hollow

desc:
These hills rise and fall gradually as they stretch in every direction except
west and east, where they fall to blend into grassy fields. To the south passage
is blocked by taller hills that rise up from steep, vertical rock faces; the
rock is crumbling, and too unstable to climb. This area is marked by clumps of
low-growing grasses of various heights, creating a three-dimensional patchwork
mosaic. No mature trees grow here; some bare areas on the sides of the hills
where bare rock has been exposed suggest that the dominance of low-growing
vegetation is due to thin soil.
name: Low, Heath-covered Hills

desc:
These hills are low and rounded, rolling off into the distance in the east and
south. A continuous line of vertical cuts in the hillsides to the north and west
break this monotony, creating steep drops of crumbling stone that are too tall
to traverse, and too unstable to climb. A great number of heath shrubs grow
here, covering the hills in a low patchwork of vegetation. Gaps in this coverage
are filled in by low, clump-forming grasses, creating a mix of tan and green
that blends with distance. There are no mature trees here; some exposed, rocky
outcroppings suggest that the soil is too thin.
name: Hilly Heath

desc:
Heath-covered hills stretch in all directions, with exposed white rock showing
through on the sharper angles. Closer inspection of the rock finds it to be
crumbly and dry. In deeper cracks, leeward of the northern wind, scrub pines
grow short and twisted. On the far northern and southern edges of these hills,
cliffs drop off, high and treacherous.
name: Rolling, Heath-covered Hills

desc:
Coarse grasses roll on hills that heighten to the north and east, then drop off
in sharp white cliffs. In low dips, the hills fill with spiky, green and yellow
gorse, and rarely, a red-berried rowan grows bent and gnarly. South, the hills
decline and the grass turns from rough, tan sedge into a green valley.
name: Coarse-grassed Hills

desc:
Scabious and hay rattle fill this vast, low valley with periwinkle and yellow
flowers as it pitches down from distant hills to the east. The air is filled
with fresh, green scents, and long-tailed tits fly about in small flocks hunting
for moths and butterflies.
name: Green Valley

desc:
This green valley gives a grand view of the land as it slopes up, east, slowly
reaching far off hills. Grasses billow in gentle breezes, with yellow flowers
dipping as small bees, wasps and flies hover towards their nectar. To the west,
in the deepest part of the valley, great elm trees grow among thick gorse.
name: Green Valley

desc:
Muted orange and tan grasses cover this low, shallow valley as it slopes down
south and east. To the south, the land is rife with green and yellow shrubs, and
to the east the valley stretches as far as the eye can see and is strewn with
large, scattered pine trees. North and west, stunning white cliffs obtrude over
the land.
name: Low, Grassy Valley

desc:
This shallow valley transitions from spiky, green shrubs in the west to purple,
flowering grasses. The air is filled with bees and butterflies, and rarely an
orange-breasted bird flies off. Overall, the land steadily rises to the east
until it rolls into distant hills.
name: Shallow Valley

desc:
This lush field is thick with sweet smells, rising up from meadowsweet that
carpets the land. The ground is almost alive, bouncing back every step, and the
air is riotous with insects and birds. To the east, the land rises slowly, and
is interspersed with large elm trees. West, the land is pressed by a large,
hanging cliff. South, the land turns from lush to boggy.
name: Lush Field

desc:
In this brushy valley, slick-barked buckthorn stick their thorny, black-berried
branches up through green and yellow gorse in an ancient, verdant battle. In the
deepest point of the valley, east, large elm trees grow, striking to the eye.
Underfoot, skeletal shrubs, dead grasses and old weeds hinder travel.
name: Brushy Valley

desc:
Small-leaved, yellow-flowered shrubs fill this low, wide valley as it rises up
to the south. The shrubs form in close, and stick their spiky leaves out on
club-like branches. The ground is uneven and rocky with hummocks and dips hidden
in tangles of old, dense mats of grass. To the west, the shrubs grow wild and
imposing, blocking travel that way.
name: Brushy Valley

desc:
Rolling hills of various heights are visible to the north and west, although
passage in those directions is blocked by nearly continuous cuts in the ground.
These cuts mark sudden dropoffs, near-vertical hillsides of exposed rock that
are steep and tall enough to prevent travel. Vegetation here is low-growing but
plentiful; these hills are covered by grasses and short heath shrubs. In some
areas the grass is very thin, the exposed soil giving way to bare rock that has
been worn smooth by weather over many years.
name: Hilly Heath

desc:
This valley is wide and flat, with the edges of the valley bowl rising up to the
north and west in the form of rolling hills. A small stream twists through the
valley, originating in an exposed cut of rock at the edge of the hills; the
water flows even in dry weather, suggesting that a natural spring is the source.
The stream bed is wide but shallow; the bottom is a mix of gravel and mud, and
is easily stirred up to cloud the water with sediment. Tall brush grows along
the edges of the stream, with an occasional tree rising above the tangle.
Farther away from the stream bed the brush thins out to tall grasses, which mat
down underfoot.
name: Source of a Stream in the Hills

desc:
Low, rolling hills rise above the plains to the west and a valley to the
southeast. Steep cuts mar the hills in a number of places, including a long line
of near-continuous cuts to the west and north that reveal steep, crumbling rock
faces on the sides of the hills. These rock faces are tall enough to prevent
movement in those directions, and unstable enough to make them too dangerous to
climb. Low grasses grow in clumps of various heights here, with short heath
growing above them in a patchwork pattern. Here and there rock has been exposed,
suggesting that the soil is thin and poor.
name: Low, Heath-covered Hills

desc:
Hills rise up in all directions except east, where the valley descends as it
continues. The hills appear at this distance to be covered with sparse
vegetation, but in this low area the vegetation is lush and thick. Tall grasses
grow in clumps, interspersed with some thickets of tall gorse bushes, and the
occasional small tree rises above these. Some threads of greener grass cut
through these fields, winding ribbons of brilliant colour in an otherwise muted
landscape: closer inspection shows these to be drainage channels through the
valley, and they likely stay wet long after a rain.
name: Low, Grassy Valley

desc:
These low, rolling hills are covered with short brush, both heath and young
gorse bushes. These bushes hide narrow clefts and shallow depressions, hazards
for those passing through here on foot as well as for those that are mounted.
Some areas contain little to no vegetation, and on closer examination the soil
appears to be extremely thin: bare rock is exposed in some of these areas,
light-coloured and crumbling. North and south there are sudden, vertical cuts
through the hills, steep and wide enough to block movement in those directions.
To the west the low heath gives way to denser brush, and the tangled bushes,
vines and thorns block the descent to the plains below.
name: Hilly Heath

desc:
These rolling hills extend to the east, forming a border to the valley that
slopes down to the north. South and west the hills are interrupted by abrupt
cuts and sudden drops, with exposed, crumbling stone ledges that make travel
impossible and climbing too dangerous to attempt. The vegetation here is
low-growing, with no trees in sight: grass clumps of varying heights cover the
ground, and small shrubs rise above the grasses, both heath and scraggly gorse.
Bare rock is exposed on some of the slopes throughout these hills, suggesting
that the soil here is thin.
name: Low, Heath-covered Hills

desc:
These hills are steep, forming the edge of a wide, flat valley that extends to
the north. To the south a continuous line of steep dropoffs prevents travel; the
drop is not far, but the exposed, crumbling rock makes traversing to the plains
below unsafe. Vegetation here is low-growing, consisting of clumps of grasses of
various heights and a number of broadleaf forbs. No mature trees are visible
among these hills; judging from the bare rock that is frequently exposed on the
hillsides, a thin soil restricts the height of the vegetation. Drainage cuts and
channels run in branching patterns through the hills towards the plains below,
the gaps wide and deep.
name: Steep, Hilly Heath

desc:
These hills are low and rolling, extending to the west and north. The low spots
between the rises are brushy, full of tall grasses and dense vegetation. The
ground in these low areas stays wet long after a rain, and some small stands of
river birch have begun to rise up above the surrounding shrubs, thriving in the
moist soil. Higher up on the hills rock is exposed, indicating the soil is
thinner and drier; clumps of short grasses and the occasional low-growing shrub
are the only things growing in these spots. To the south are steep dropoffs, the
exposed rock crumbling and too unsafe to climb or traverse. To the east the
hills descend quickly into a brushy marsh.
name: Brush-covered Hills

desc:
This overgrown fen is very wet, being low and collecting water from hills to the
west. Rushes and reeds grow tall and hide sulphurous bogs and green pools. The
land sinks in each step, drawing any land bound creature into the quagmire, slow
and mucky. The land is so low that only to the west can one climb out, there
being a gradual abatement of wetness.
name: Low, Overgrown Fen

desc:
This valley is wide and flat, the edges rising up into hills to the south and
east. Tall grasses dominate this area, their feathery seed heads bending with
the slightest breeze. Dead stems and flower heads from previous years mix with
the current green growth, creating a duller, lighter colour when viewed at a
distance. Some shrubs are mixed in among the grasses, and here and there a young
pine tree has established itself. Deer trails have worn the grass down to dirt
and dead stems in places, revealing a rich, dark soil underfoot.
name: Low, Grassy Valley

desc:
Whortle-leaved shrubs hide in the dips of these low, rolling, heath-topped
hills. Running through southeast, a small stream cuts deep into the ground,
exposing bedrock and flows clear, showing its gravelly bottom. Where the stream
sits at ground level, dark-leaved willows gather, small and stiff with
papery-thin, bluish leaves.
name: Small Stream in the Hills

desc:
This cut under a rock ledge opens into a wider burrow, with rock overhead and a
dirt floor. The burrow is not tall, and anyone except a hobbit would have to
stoop to move around. There are some shallow depressions in the dirt floor, and
many of them are filled with organic debris, mostly old grass stems and dead
weed stalks. The rock at the back of the burrow has formed into a series of
step-like ledges, with narrow but deep alcoves extending beyond each into the
back wall of the cave. The air in here is damp, but there is little moisture
visible other than some small beaded water droplets on the rock ceiling.
name: Rocky Outcropping

desc:
This verdant meadow sweeps all about, the vetch and knapweed providing a green
and purple, rippling view. A rich smell of life pervades the air, and insects
buzz about, emboldened by their boundless numbers. Concealed in the grasses is a
trail that runs generally north and south, muddy and narrow. To the west, grand
oak trees peek over a wild, impenetrable bramble-wall.
name: Verdant Meadow

desc:
A purple-tinged meadow stretches about, the flowered grasses blowing gently in
the breeze. White butterflies and small, yellow moths flutter lazily, dodging
quick wagtails. The ground is spongy, and gets quite wet on the eastern side of
this land. Running north and south through the meadow, a narrow, muddy trail
meanders. To the west stands a striking forest, impassable with thorny bramble.
name: Purple-tinged Meadow

desc:
Horse-high goat willow grows rangy, dark-stemmed, and thick in this boggy land.
Green, oval leaves grow in all directions as the shrubs close in on each other,
vying for sunlight. A narrow trail snakes its way through the land, north and
south, but is easily lost as the verdure grows quick to overtake the open space,
creating a maze for all but the most vigilant. East, the willow shrubs grow so
thick as to impede travel and give life to a colony of large, vivid, purple
butterflies that fly about the shrub tops, flitting and wry.
name: Goat Willow Maze

desc:
This deciduous forest is mostly oak, but some birch and rowan trees are mixed
throughout. Here and there the skeletal remains of a pine tree still stands,
shaded into oblivion, branches bare and broken. Many of the living trees here
look unhealthy, with dying and broken limbs that mirror those of the pines; some
have succumbed to whatever is afflicting them. The bark on these is sloughing
off in long strips, revealing a white threadlike fungus underneath. Piles of
bark and dead, broken branches have collected beneath these trees, mixed with
the leaf litter that covers the forest floor.
name: Diseased Oak and Birch Forest

desc:
This mature forest is populated with large trees, their trunks wide and devoid
of branches until they spread out far overhead in a dense canopy that blocks the
sky. Small saplings are trying to grow in this deep shade, scattered at random
among the larger trunks. The occasional dead tree is still standing, but the
canopy has already closed around the space its leafless branches left; others
have fallen, either lying on the ground or wedged at an angle where they landed
across the forked trunks of those still standing. Low-growing plants form clumps
across the forest floor, which is otherwise devoid of brush.
name: Mature Oak and Birch Trees

desc:
A small but well-defined trail extends westward into the wood. To the east, the
trail becomes faint and harder to follow as it descends toward a deeply carved
valley. Bird song dominates the aural atmosphere, interrupted occasionally by
distant, guttural squeals likely originating from the infamous swineherd that
wanders Chetwood.
name: Sloping Trail

desc:
A small trail meanders through openings in the forest where fallen trees grow
moss. The dense greenery does not hinder the birds above or the squirrels near
the ground. Saplings and younger trees crowd the trail's edges until it opens as
it approaches the settlement of Staddle to the south.
name: Woodland Trail North of Staddle

desc:
The ground is quite moist here and the small stones and brushwood underfoot are
covered with a soft, yellowish green moss. Elm and alder trees grow all around
and begin to crowd together as the western landscape transitions into dense
forest. To the east, the reed-filled edge of the Midgewater Marshes is just
visible, bordered on its southern edge by hillsides.
name: Sparse Wood

desc:
The forest is lightly populated by oaks and yews. The thick, knobby trunks and
reddish bark of the latter trees seem ominous, as if an ancient and inscrutable
spirit lurks within. Distant sounds of foot and hoof traffic to the south
occasionally break the eerie aura permeating this edge of the wood.
name: Southern Chetwood

desc:
Not too far to the north, the Chetwood becomes a dense and impenetrable tangle
of brushwood, tall ferns, and old thick-trunked trees. In the immediate
surroundings, however, only a few dozen oaks stand along the northern edge of
the Old East Road. Charred grasses dot the space between trees, likely the
remnants of recent camps made by travellers coming and going from Bree.
name: Light Forest Bordering the Old East Road

desc:
The surrounding forest is dominated by a single, massive yew tree. Smaller oaks
and dogwoods appear scattered in the distance, their branches hanging low as if
bending the knee to the kingly, ancient tree. The ground is littered with bright
red arils that contrast starkly with the dull forest floor.
name: Base of an Enormous Yew

desc:
The surrounding field explodes in colour as wildflowers of every hue and season
reach toward the open sky. Toward the east, the flowers fade away as reeds
spring stiffly from the soil. Piles of chopped wood and logs lie to the south,
seemingly abandoned.
name: Field of Wildflowers

desc:
A narrow field, full of wildflowers and tufts of grasses both short and tall,
stretches between the eerily dark Chetwood forest to the west and the muggy
Midgewater Marshes to the east. A cacophony of croaks and chirps echoes from the
marshes and across the meadow.
name: Open Meadow

desc:
This field extends north and south, marking the boundary between the dense,
Chetwood forest to the west and the Midgewater Marshes to the east. The trail
bends away from the forest toward a narrow field in the north. Tall tufts of
switchgrass line the trail, becoming thicker and more crowded nearer the
marshes.
name: Bend in the Forest Trail

desc:
A large boulder blocks the violent current and creates a bay of relatively calm
water. The water is not very deep while the ground is quite slippery from algae
growing underwater. Nearby there is some sort of small, stony beach. Even the
faintest light, created by algae or moss, bounces off the moist surfaces and
creates shimmering effects all around.
name: Subterranean Torrent

desc:
Strong whirls, caused by the current, have formed here in this narrow
underground stream. Both the ceiling and walls are covered in algae which grows
wildly in the ever present moisture. The ground has been polished smooth by the
force of the water and offer nearly no traction.
name: Subterranean Torrent

desc:
Numerous stones and boulders of varying sizes and shapes cover the river's bank
of hard rock. The walls are covered in a brownish, very wet slime while long
patches of algae or green fungi hang from the ceiling. The ceiling is formed
from jagged rock, dripping with condensation that has collected on its surface.
name: Subterranean Beach

desc:
This field occupies the land between the town of Bree and a well-travelled trail
to the south. Bree-hill dominates the northern horizon, surrounded by an
enormous hedge behind a deeply-dug dike. To the east, the outskirts of the
Chetwood forest appear on the southern edge of Old East Road, though the path in
that direction is blocked by a tangle of thorny bushes and large boulders.
name: Southern Base of Bree-hill

desc:
The southern end of the Chetwood forest continues west and east with the Old
East Road cutting through it to the north. The forest gives way to grass-covered
downs to the south. A small, modest home lies to the north.
name: Quiet Glade

desc:
The trees grow thick and tight together, their branches intertwining overhead.
The Old East Road can be seen to the north, and a careful navigation through the
tree trunks could grant access to the thoroughfare. To the south, a field can be
seen below some rolling hills to the southeast.
name: Southern Chetwood

desc:
The surrounding forest is fairly dense, but some light permeates the canopy from
the north where the Old East Road cuts through Chetwood. To the south, the trees
disappear and give way to plains and then the Downs farther in the distance.
name: Southern Chetwood

desc:
A light forest borders the Old East Road which lies just beyond a dense hedgerow
in the north. Maples and alders grow somewhat spaced apart here on the outskirts
of the forest but crowd more closely together in the east and south.
name: Southern Chetwood

desc:
This is a sturdy wooden house, obviously built for and by a master lumberjack.
It is small, modestly decorated, and impeccably tidy. A fireplace and shelves
line the northern wall, and a small, neatly made bed rests under a picture
window to the west.
name: Lumberjack's Hut

desc:
Tall trees grow fairly dense here in this part of the Chetwood forest. Fallen
trunks block most off-path travel, and dense branches obscure the way to the Old
East Road to the north. The only path extends east and west, deeper into the
wood or out into an open field.
name: Corner of the Chetwood Forest

desc:
The trail winds through the grass and little bushes that thrive here. The air is
filled with the delicious aroma of life, as insects buzz around, emboldened by
their vast numbers. Tall trees obscure the view to the west, where the trail
appears to vanish. To the north, the Old East Road is hidden behind an
impenetrable hedge.
name: Trail at the Forest's Edge

desc:
From here, the trail runs through a grassy field to the west and east. A thick
hedge blocks the trail to the north, allowing only the small critters that can
scamper beneath it to pass. The fields become more rough and sloping to the
south.
name: Fields along the Old East Road

desc:
An large mighty oak tree grows surrounded by thick brushwood. It's trunk is
light green with various mosses growing up its length. The ground surrounding it
is muddy, with dense brushwood seemingly falling down into the depths of the
ground, slowly being eaten up by the earth.
name: At a Large, Mighty Oak

desc:
Dense young trees grow tightly together, their narrow trunks shooting straight
up into the sky, each vying to get as much sunlight as possible. The ground is
littered with debris, branches lying on top of each other several inches thick,
interspersed with dead and dying vegetation.
name: Brushwood

desc:
Ancient trees dot the landscape, their massive branches dripping with emerald
foliage. Underneath each tree are bushes with short thorny branches that appear
to defend the tree from anything substantial approaching without being
scratched.
name: Brushwood

desc:
Bushes grow densely throughout the area, making it difficult to move; their
branches sharp and prickly. Weeds grow wherever they are able, taking advantage
to the slightest opening throughout the bushes. The scent of pollen is heavy in
the air.
name: Brushwood

desc:
The high grass continues in all directions. Occasionally, patches of rocks and
stones can be seen. In some places, the soil is so devoid of nutrients that only
the sparsest of grasses and only a few low and brown plants are able to take
root. Dry, prickly weeds seem to be the only plants that flourish on the rocky
outcrops.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
Tall grass grows all around, rustling with the slight breezes that blow across
the plain from time to time. Nothing blocks the view to the horizon in any
direction. Sometimes, small formations break the monotony of the plains and add
some contrast to the vast grassy sea.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
The Old East Road looks as if it has been in use for many ages past. The hard
stones are very worn, yet they long strong enough to withstand many ages to
come. In some places, a stone is cracked right through the middle. However, most
of the stones are well intact.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The passage of thousands of travellers has polished the inner tracks of this
ancient road. The borders however have started to break down into the
surrounding turf. In the very centre of the path only a few cracks are present
testifying to the craftsmanship.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The old road becomes a bit narrower as it leads around a small depression then
widens again behind it. The surface of the road is made of dark stones cut into
squares. Between the sharp edges of the stones, thin bits of grass grow in the
cracks.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Old East Road leads through the mostly flat landscape. At the road's edges,
nature starts to take back what was taken a long time ago. Bushes, tall grass
and trees stand along the endless line of paved stones which form the road and
which lie here for more years than are to count.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Running almost perfectly straight for miles, the Old East Road seems to be more
a drawn line than something that has been built. The ancient trees lining the
road make it appear to be more an avenue of kings rather than a travellers road
through the wilderness.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Small tufts of yellow grass grows close to the ground, never reaching but a few
inches off of the ground. Spiky bushes dot the landscape, their branches
sparsely covered in dried leaves. The ground is dry and dusty between the meagre
plants.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
All around, high bushes in shades of light red hinder the view. The stems of the
plants are slender and the leaves quite evenly spread. In some areas patches of
green, thorny bushes struggle to take root in between the other plants.
name: Brushwood

desc:
The ground is incredibly compacted, from the markings of hooved animals. Their
grazing creating vast meandering trails throughout the grasslands. The bushes
have lost all of their leaves; only the trees with their high branches still
have any leaves available.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
The plain is covered with low bushes. Many of these plants have slender branches
with green and yellow leaves, which look similar to a three-fingered hand. Only
the occasional trees rise above those bushes. From time to time, a bush with
leaves red as the setting sun adds a few bright spots of colour.
name: Brushwood

desc:
Seemingly without end, the grasses and low bushes covering the plain stretch out
in all directions. Many mounds and holes dot the landscape, dug by small,
burrowing animals who have claimed the land as their homes. A dense patch of
brush sits on the western horizon.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
Thick briars and brambles form an impassable wall of brush to the north. The
ground here is deplete of anything alive, covered only in dead vegetation or
bare earth. Large trees grow here sporadically about, some close enough to each
other that their branches wrap around each other.
name: Twisted Trees in Some Brushwood

desc:
The even soil is covered with many fallen and half rotten trees; over them the
brushwood grows dense and repairs the damage the falling trees caused to the
lower vegetation. It nearly seems a small forest has withered and died to make
space for some other kind of vegetation. Many of the bushes, vines, and thorny
scrubs prospering here seem to have grown just within the last few years.
name: Twisted Trees in Some Brushwood

desc:
Thick brushwood and a few groups of trees grow on this plain, hiding the soil
from view in most places. Many trace signs of animals can be seen here as marks
of digging, nibbled leaves, stems and trunks, piles of droppings, and the
occasional animal-path through the brushwood.
name: Brushwood

desc:
The branches of the old tree offer safe footing, and the rough, dark brown bark
provide good handholds for climbers. In between the leaves, the surrounding
landscape can be seen, mostly thick brushwood. In one of the higher tree
perches, the remains of a disrupted nest of birds can be spotted.
name: Tree Top

desc:
High in the top of this old tree, the view over the surrounding brushwood is
excellent. Brushwood, grass, and other trees of all colours and shapes stretch
out in all directions. Whether the occasional movement seen below in a bush is
from the local wildlife, some traveller, or just the wind, however, cannot be
judged. A man-made, sturdy rope bridge has been constructed here, stretching to
connect a tree to the east.
name: High in a Tree

desc:
Rising not only above the landscape but also above all of the other trees, this
place gives an excellent view. The branches of the tree's crown are not very
thick or strong, but there are many of them, which makes finding a safe position
for feet and hands an easy task. A sturdy rope bridge stretches across to
another tree in the distance to the west.
name: High in a Tree

desc:
The branches here are not very thick or heavy, but they are plentiful. Finding
safe footing should be no issue. The tree top provides a vantage point of the
surrounding landscape, with the Old East Road to the north and the bustling town
of Bree to the east.
name: Tree Top

desc:
The trail beneath your feet is cluttered with splintered logs, sawdust, and pine
needles. It curves to the west before resuming its northerly path. To the east,
a recently cut coppice lies off the road, while to the south, the trail connects
with the Old East Road.
name: Logging Trail

desc:
To the south and west, this trail is surrounded by dense foliage. Many animal
traces can be spotted here, such as digging marks, nibbled leaves, stems, and
trunks. On the outskirts of Combe, the trees are smaller and younger. The air is
thick with the scent of damp wood, pine needles, and sawdust.
name: Trail Heading toward Combe

desc:
Unlike the rest of the wood, which is densely forested with wild buckthorn,
dogwood, aspen, and spruce, this little grove is devoid of variation. Hazelnut
trees, which are unusual elsewhere in Chetwood, are plentiful and spaced among
the oaks. The forest floor is loamy, with small, dark black boulders scattered
about.
name: Grove of Hazelnut and Oak

desc:
Given its location in one of Chetwood's most remote areas, one could reasonably
presume that this acreage is densely forested. Despite this, there are only a
few young oaks to be seen. An indication of a former arboreal life can be seen
in the large number of short, decaying stumps that are scattered throughout the
low grasses and profusion of wildflowers.
name: Mature and Overgrown Coppice

desc:
Many of the trees in this part of the forest are young, evidenced by their
narrow trunks under the low, lacy canopy. Occasional stumps appear among the
undergrowth, many of them covered in assemblages of white mushrooms, appearing
like pale, fleshy scales interspersed among the decaying bark.
name: Young Wood

desc:
The forest dips and rolls as low hills stretch in all directions. At the bottom
of a few of the hills, shallow trenches appear at the base of some of the
younger hazels and oaks, and the underbrush appears crushed, as if tread on by a
pack of heavy-footed creatures.
name: Lightly-wooded Hillside

desc:
The surrounding forest is light and slopes gently toward a field in the south.
Beyond that, Bree-hill can be seen rising above the landscape and bisected by
the Old East Road. The forest extends to the east where a number of felled trees
lie waiting to be transported.
name: Light, Sloped Forest

desc:
This area has been recently deforested; only the remaining stumps show that
there once was a forest here. The forest floor is overgrown by brushwood and
thistle, and where the bare ground appears, tracks of long-gone lumberjacks and
their horses can still be easily identified.
name: Small Clear-cutting

desc:
From the highest branches of the mighty yew, a breathtaking vista extends in
every direction. The great Chetwood forest sprawls for many miles to the north
and west. The well-travelled Old East Road appears far off in the southern
distance. And an enormous, murky marsh stretches toward the eastern horizon as
far as the eye can see.
name: Top of an Enormous Yew

desc:
This part of the forest seems rarely travelled as passage to the west, leading
deeper into the forest, and to the east, leading out of it, is blocked by large
felled trees. Massive, lacy webs stretch between the trunks of oaks and young
yew trees, collecting dewdrops and small insects.
name: Eastern Border of Chetwood Forest

desc:
The wood finally begins to spread out, as the landscape yields toward the open
meadow in the east. Despite its visibility, a pile of downed trunks and gathered
brushwood looks to impede any access to the north and east, whereas travelling
through the forest to the west and south appears to be straightforward.
name: Edge of Chetwood Forest

desc:
Here in the heart of Chetwood, the branches of great, ancient oaks are so
complexly entwined that it is impossible to tell one tree from another. The
canopy seems a wooly green blanket overhead that blocks the light and warmth of
the sun and creates an unexpected chill in the air.
name: Heart of the Forest

desc:
The forest trail is thronged on both sides by tall trees with trunks both wide
and thin, and the thick undergrowth at the base of the trees makes any off-trail
travel extremely precarious. Silence hangs palpably in the air: heavy and
oppressive. Each rare, innocuous sound that erupts from this silence, a bird
chirping or a rodent burrowing in the undergrowth, is transformed into an eerie,
muffled echo as it reverberates around the woods.
name: Trail through the Depths of Chetwood

desc:
The landscape transitions quickly from thick forest to open field. To the west,
a narrow trail weaves its way through the trees, quickly disappearing into the
dark depths of Chetwood. Low dogwood trees dot the forest edges, taking
advantage of the thinning canopy. The trail continues east toward a grassy
meadow, punctuated by a few ancient oaks.
name: Trail at the Edge of Chetwood Forest

desc:
These woods are filled with mature oak and birch trees, with a multitude of
smaller, thin-trunked trees below. There are some pine trees mixed in, but they
seem to be struggling in the shade cast by the larger deciduous trees: the pines
are still green near their tops, but below that they are skeletal and bare, with
broken stumps of branches sticking out from their trunks at regular intervals.
Here and there a giant clump of dirt rises over a wide depression, the root mass
of a fallen tree that was torn up when the tree fell. The forest floor is mostly
bare, covered with fallen leaves from past seasons, and too shaded for smaller
plants to grow.
name: Mature Oak and Birch Trees

desc:
This forest is populated by mature trees, mostly oak with some birch mixed in.
Small clumps of pines are interspersed, struggling to compete with their
deciduous neighbours: their trunks are bare up to the very top, where tufts of
green needles reach for the available light. The canopy above is almost
unbroken, creating a deep twilight even during the day. The occasional sunfleck
makes it to the ground when the sun is up, lighting the small leafy plants that
grow among the leaf litter and fallen branches. Other than these small plants
the forest floor is open, lacking obstructive undergrowth.
name: Mature Oak and Birch Forest

desc:
These woods are populated by many mature oak trees, the trunks broad and
broadly-spaced. Despite the room between the trees, their canopies meet
overhead, forming a solid unbroken ceiling of branches and leaves that blocks
the sun from reaching the ground. A number of fallen trees, most mid-sized or
smaller, lie strewn across the forest floor, skeletal remnants of those that
failed to reach up through the canopy above to compete for the available light.
Very few plants grow among the fallen trunks and leaf litter that covers the
ground, owing to this lack of light.
name: Dense Oak Woods

desc:
The wood surrounding the forest trail is wild and secluded. Trees and brushwood
have grown so densely at its edges that not a single sunbeam finds its way down
to the ground, and it is hardly possible to tell whether it is day or night. The
surrounding oaks are covered in moss, growing like a green pelt across their
wide trunks. A smell of damp and rotting wood fills the air.
name: Trail through the Heart of Chetwood Forest

desc:
This dense, mature forest is composed mostly of deciduous trees, oak and birch
and the occasional rowan. Some pine trees grow here too, but seem to be
struggling in the shade cast by their taller broadleaved neighbours: they are
bare up to their tops, where a small cone of green needles remains. Here and
there one of these pines seems to have died completely, leaving a tall skeletal
form whose trunk is ghostly white where the bark has started to peel off. The
ground underfoot is uneven, with raised hummocks and shallow depressions, and at
the base of one such hummock there is a small hole between exposed tree roots.
To the north and west the trees begin to thin out, but the increased light
reaching the ground has allowed dense brush to grow, creating a tangled barrier
that prevents movement in those directions.
name: Towering Oak Trees

desc:
A large pine grove emerges in the middle of this forest. The pines have grown
densely enough here that the surrounding deciduous trees weren't able to
establish among them, although the edges of the grove are being slowly
encroached upon by the surrounding oak and rowan. The pines are bare most of the
way up, with a dense canopy of green needles only at the tops where they are
exposed to the sun. The ground below them takes on a reddish tint from the years
of accumulated needles that they have shed, and is soft and spongy to walk upon.
Not much grows beneath these trees, owing to the lack of light that makes it all
the way down to the forest floor.
name: Pine Grove in the Forest

desc:
This mature deciduous forest is composed primarily of oak, the trees growing
closely enough together that the canopy overhead is unbroken. Some tall pine
trees are mixed in, but they are doing poorly in the shade cast by the oaks:
their tops are green and full of needles, but the trunks below are bare and
skeletal, with broken branches and peeling bark. The undergrowth here is
relatively sparse due to the lack of light reaching the forest floor, with only
the occasional thin, scraggly bush or clump of low-growing herbaceous plants
visible among the leaf litter from the trees above.
name: Dense Oak Woods

desc:
This dense, mature forest is primarily oak, but some pine trees are mixed in
beneath the canopy. These pines appear to be struggling to compete for light:
they are mostly bare, with tufts of green needles at their tops. The branches
below are thin, sticking out from each trunk in regular intervals like spokes on
a wheel; many are broken and have lost their bark, appearing white where the
wood underneath shows through. Some of the oak trees here are bare and dying as
well: bark is sloughing off their trunks, revealing spidery white webs of fungus
underneath. Some have fallen, and the trunks are overrun with honey-coloured
fruiting bodies of the fungus that is consuming them.
name: Diseased Oak Forest

desc:
These dense, mature woods are a mix of oak and pine. The pine trees grow in
clumps, and are green near the top but bare and dead looking below, where the
soft needle-like foliage has been shaded until it dropped. Most of the side
branches on these pines have long since broken off, leaving short stumps
sticking out from otherwise bare trunks, and making passage between them easy.
Here and there one of the pines has died completely, shaded into oblivion by the
oaks towering above, and the bark has peeled off to leave a ghostly white trunk.
There are a number of fallen trees in this part of the forest, and a
particularly large trunk appears to have rotted into hollow sections.
name: Dense Woods

desc:
The surrounding forest opens upon a wide meadow, its edges lined by the tall,
thin trunks of golden aspens. Sloping slightly downward into the lea, the trail
rises again in the distance. The air smells of wild mint and damp soil, and the
buzzing sounds of tiny insects flitting along in the low grasses fill the lea
with a low, continuous hum.
name: Trail through an Aspen-edged Lea

desc:
The forest encroaches from all directions except the east. Thick trunked maples
are dwarfed by giant spruce. Large pinecones blanket the ground below, making it
hard to find stable footing. The air smells of pine needles and the rot of leaf
litter.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The trees are quite dense in this area and many of them grow very closely to one
another. A number of maidenhair ferns spread lushly underfoot, and some of the
areas between the trees are carpeted with brushwood. The thick vegetation makes
it very hard to move deeper into the forest.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The trees appear older in this part of the wood, their trunks and branches
thicker than the trees along the trail to the west. Strange shadows move in the
branches and within the undergrowth; it is difficult to tell whether they are
the silhouettes of forest creatures or a trick of the light.
name: Heart of Chetwood Forest

desc:
The trees crowd so tightly together in the heart of Chetwood that it appears as
if they are conspiring against unsuspecting travellers. Their mossy trunks
provide an ambient dampness and lingering odour of rot. Even during the day, the
canopy overhead is practically unbroken, producing a deep twilight.
name: Heart of Chetwood Forest

desc:
Mature pine, oak, and beech trees grow in the vicinity while tender saplings
struggle to rise from the leafy humus below. Travel westward is obstructed by a
massive escarpment whose rocky base transforms into a forested hillside
overhead.
name: Before a Large Escarpment

desc:
In the centre of a small glade surrounded by a dense stand of trees, a curious
structure appears. On the ground, dozens of slim, downed trunks are organised in
a wide circle with their tops leaning against each other and forming a primitive
shelter.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
A low hill, densely forested with towering oaks and pines, rolls away from the
high, steep ridge to the west. Birds can occasionally be observed scouting for
food in the branches. The wood stops abruptly to the south at a harsh, unnatural
border, beyond which a field of wildflowers spreads to the horizon.
name: Wooded Hillside

desc:
From the outside, this makeshift shelter appears to be on the verge of collapse.
But inside, the structure is obviously much sturdier. Recently felled logs line
the inner walls, and a mat of slender, woven roots and lichen is laid upon the
floor. A little patch of ground behind the shelter has been fostered into a
small, private herbal garden, where several unusual and exotic plants flourish,
infusing the air with a peculiar tranquil odour.
name: Crude Shelter

desc:
Covered by broad swaths of short grass and the occasional shrub, this field
transitions to forest in the north while a small trail that runs through the
village of Staddle appears in the far eastern distance. To the south, the
landscape slopes upward toward Bree-hill and dozens of hobbit holes appear as
dark specks on the eastern side of the hill.
name: Field North of Bree-hill

desc:
Here, the forest protrudes from the progression of low hills that spread across
Chetwood, forming a narrow ridgeline high above a valley to the south. On its
western, eastern, and southern sides, the ridge features steep, rocky slopes,
graced by only the most resilient of trees that lean at precarious angles but

name: Forested Ridge

desc:
A small fissure of the earth, eroded over time, has created this crevice. A
thick carpet of moss conceals it from light and wind, making the air feel cool,
but also damp and sticky. The base of the crevice collects rainwater that seeps
in from the edges of the fissure, and it has become a breeding ground for small,
biting insects.
name: Watery Crevice
